stem: A 35-year-old construction worker is brought to the emergency department after falling approximately 10 feet from scaffolding and landing on his feet with his right leg extended and abducted. He immediately felt severe pain in the groin and hip and was unable to walk or bear weight. He denies head trauma or loss of consciousness. He has no prior orthopedic history and is otherwise healthy. No medications or substance use are reported. On physical examination, he is alert and hemodynamically stable. The right lower extremity appears shortened and is held in a position of abduction and external rotation at the hip. There is no swelling or deformity of the thigh. Distal pulses are intact and equal bilaterally. Sensation and motor strength are preserved distally in the affected limb. There is tenderness over the anterior hip, and passive movement of the joint elicits pain. Radiographs are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with a shortened, externally rotated, and abducted limb following a fall with the leg extended—hallmarks of an anterior hip dislocation. Although less common than posterior dislocations, anterior dislocations typically occur when force is applied to an extended, externally rotated, and abducted leg, as in this case when the patient landed awkwardly from height. The femoral head is forced anteriorly out of the acetabulum and often ends up inferior to the pubic bone. The physical exam findings of external rotation and abduction—without thigh deformity or neurologic compromise—are the key features distinguishing this injury. Radiographs are essential for confirmation, but USMLE often test diagnostic reasoning from limb positioning. The absence of internal rotation rules out posterior dislocation, and the mechanism fits anterior displacement. The normal distal neurovascular exam helps exclude complications like femoral artery injury or sciatic nerve compromise. Early diagnosis and reduction are important to avoid long-term complications such as avascular necrosis or post- traumatic arthritis. On exam, posterior dislocation classically causes internal rotation, whereas anterior dislocation causes external rotation.

subject: MSK
stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off a bunk bed and striking his head on the hardwood floor from a height of approximately 6 feet. His mother reports that he briefly lost consciousness but awoke within a few minutes and has since been drowsy and minimally responsive. On examination, his Glasgow Coma Scale score is 10. He has bilateral periorbital ecchymoses and retroauricular bruising behind the left ear. Clear fluid is observed draining from his left nostril and right ear. His vital signs are: temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure 152/96 mm Hg, heart rate 48/min, and respiratory rate 8/min. Noncontrast CT of the head is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with multiple classic findings associated with a basilar skull fracture, a serious traumatic injury involving the base of the skull. The presence of Battle sign (postauricular ecchymosis) and raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis) strongly suggests fractures involving the petrous temporal bone and anterior cranial fossa, respectively. In addition, the clear fluid draining from the ear and nose is most consistent with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) otorrhea and rhinorrhea, indicating dural tear and communication between the subarachnoid space and the external environment. These physical signs are highly suggestive and should raise concern for underlying cranial base trauma. Furthermore, the child exhibits bradycardia, hypertension, and irregular respirations, a classic Cushing triad, which reflects elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) and impending herniation. This is a physiologic response aimed at maintaining cerebral perfusion in the face of rising pressure. The increased ICP may result from associated cerebral edema, hemorrhage, or other intracranial injuries accompanying the skull fracture. The recognition of this triad is vital in emergency settings, as it warrants urgent intervention to prevent permanent neurological damage or death. Basilar skull fractures often occur due to blunt head trauma, especially in falls, motor vehicle accidents, or assaults. Pediatric patients are particularly susceptible due to thinner cranial bones. These fractures are difficult to detect on plain radiographs, and CT imaging is often required for diagnosis. However, the combination of clinical findings alone can be sufficient to strongly suggest the diagnosis and guide immediate management steps before imaging is available.

subject: MSK
stem: A 22-year-old man comes to clinic for a routine check-up before traveling abroad. He has no complaints. He is a healthy college student who recently began competing in local strongman events. For the past several months, he has followed a structured training program focused on short, high-intensity sets involving maximal effort lifting, with exercises such as weighted sled pushes, deadlifts, and farmer’s carries. He performs very little aerobic activity and does not participate in endurance sports. On physical exam, he is normotensive, with a muscular build and no abnormalities noted. His BMI is 26.1 kg/m². He enrolls in a study examining muscle adaptations in athletes. A quadriceps biopsy is taken and examined. Compared to sedentary controls, his sample shows adaptation consistent with resistance training. Which of the following best describes the expected histologic finding in this athlete? Increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers without new fiber formation

options.A: Decreased muscle fiber diameter with increased oxidative enzymes
options.B: Hyperplasia of muscle fibers with increased satellite cell proliferation
options.C: Increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers without new fiber formation
options.D: Increased mitochondrial content with capillary proliferation
options.E: Type I fiber predominance with increased fatigue resistance

answer_text: Increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers without new fiber formation
explanation: This healthy young man is engaged in high-intensity anaerobic training, as seen in strongman competitions. His routine emphasizes maximal strength and power over short durations, involving fast, explosive movements like sled pushes and deadlifts. This training style relies on type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers, which are adapted for brief, powerful contractions but fatigue rapidly. These fibers do not favor aerobic metabolism and are not targeted in endurance-based activity. The physiologic response of skeletal muscle to resistance training involves hypertrophy, defined as the increase in size of existing muscle fibers. This adaptation improves strength by increasing the contractile protein content of each fiber (primarily actin and myosin). Importantly, skeletal muscle in adult humans does not undergo hyperplasia—the number of muscle fibers remains unchanged. This is a key distinction tested on USMLE. Biopsy findings in such individuals typically show increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers with preserved architecture, without the formation of new fibers. Satellite cells may become active to support hypertrophy, but they do not lead to the formation of new fibers. These changes occur without increased mitochondrial content or capillary density, which are more typical of aerobic training adaptations. This adaptation improves peak strength and muscle mass but does not enhance endurance or oxidative capacity.
bottom_line: Increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers without new fiber formation

subject: MSK
stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic for evaluation of a chronic wound on the bottom of his right foot. He first noticed it approximately three weeks ago when his wife pointed out blood on his sock, although he himself had not felt any pain. He reports that for the past few years, he’s had a “tingly” or “numb” feeling in both feet, especially at night when lying in bed. Occasionally, this sensation disrupts his sleep. He denies fever, chills, or drainage from the wound. The lesion does not interfere with his ability to walk, but he mentions that over the last few months, he has had difficulty feeling the gas pedal while driving. He has no back pain or radiating leg symptoms. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus for 18 years, hypertension, dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease, and peripheral vascular disease. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago. On further questioning, the patient notes that he is not consistent with checking his feet and has not seen a podiatrist in years. He wears the same pair of shoes daily and admits they are quite worn. His current medications include metformin, insulin glargine, lisinopril, atorvastatin, and aspirin. On review of systems, he denies visual changes, recent falls, bowel or bladder incontinence, and upper extremity numbness. He has not noticed any weight loss or fatigue. His A1c at the last visit was 8.9%, and he has a history of non-healing wounds in the past. He has never had lower extremity amputations. On physical examination, he is afebrile and in no acute distress. There is a 2-cm shallow ulcer with a clean base and surrounding callus on the plantar surface beneath the first metatarsal head of the right foot. There is no erythema, swelling, or fluctuance. A probe-to-bone test is negative. Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation over the plantar surface of both feet, including the toes and heels. There is reduced vibration sense at the great toe bilaterally. Capillary refill is delayed, and dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are diminished on the right. The ankle- brachial index is 0.62 on the affected side. The patient is scheduled for foot imaging and vascular consultation. Which of the following nerves is primarily responsible for sensation to the affected area?
explanation: The lateral plantar nerve is one of the two terminal branches of the tibial nerve, which itself is a branch of the sciatic nerve. The tibial nerve travels through the tarsal tunnel, posterior to the medial malleolus, where it divides into the medial and lateral plantar nerves. The lateral plantar nerve innervates the lateral portion of the sole of the foot, including the lateral toes, lateral forefoot, and much of the ball of the foot near the fifth metatarsal and lateral half of the first metatarsal. In this patient, the plantar ulcer beneath the first metatarsal head falls within the sensory territory of both plantar nerves, but with a significant contribution from the lateral plantar nerve. This case is a classic presentation of a neuropathic ulcer in a patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. The sensory loss on monofilament testing confirms that the patient lacks protective sensation in the foot, allowing for repetitive microtrauma to go unnoticed, especially when paired with poor footwear. Plantar ulcers commonly develop at pressure points, including under the metatarsal heads, and the loss of pain perception due to nerve dysfunction prevents the patient from recognizing and offloading the area. The chronicity, absence of inflammation, and presence of callus further support a neuropathic rather than infectious etiology. Because the ulcer is on the plantar surface, involvement of a plantar nerve—in this case, the lateral plantar nerve—is most consistent. Management includes local wound care, offloading pressure using special orthotics or total contact casting, tight glycemic control, and vascular optimization due to coexisting peripheral arterial disease. The ABI of 0.62 suggests moderate ischemia, which increases the risk of poor healing and amputation. Multidisciplinary care involving endocrinology, podiatry, and vascular surgery is crucial. Preventive strategies like daily foot checks, appropriate footwear, and regular podiatric evaluation are essential to avoid recurrence.

subject: MSK
stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic after injuring his right wrist while playing soccer during lunch break. He recalls running backwards while defending a teammate, tripping over another player's foot, and falling awkwardly onto his outstretched right hand. Since the fall, he has complained of persistent pain at the base of his thumb, especially when twisting a doorknob or picking up objects. His parents report no visible swelling or bruising, but the boy has avoided using his right hand since the injury. On physical exam, his fingers move well and there’s no visible deformity. However, palpation over the anatomical snuffbox on the dorsal radial wrist elicits marked tenderness. Passive radial deviation also causes discomfort. A plain wrist X-ray is obtained and shows no fracture. Strain of which of the following tendons might also be seen in this patient?
explanation: Anatomic snuffbox tenderness after a fall on an outstretched hand is classic for a scaphoid fracture, even when the initial radiograph is normal. The tendon that forms the ulnar border of the snuffbox is the extensor pollicis longus, so strain or tenderness of this tendon may also be present. The key management point is thumb-spica immobilization with repeat imaging because missed scaphoid fractures risk avascular necrosis.

subject: MSK
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic for a routine examination. She has no current joint pain but reports occasional stiffness in her knees after sitting for extended periods. She works from home and does not engage in regular physical activity. Her BMI is 41 kg/m². She has no history of autoimmune disease, prior joint trauma, or occupational joint overuse. She has been postmenopausal for 2 years. Vital signs and physical exam are unremarkable. Which of the following is the strongest risk factor for developing osteoarthritis in this patient?
explanation: Obesity is the most potent modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of osteoarthritis (OA), particularly in weight-bearing joints such as the knees, hips, and lumbar spine. The mechanical overload caused by excess body weight accelerates the breakdown of articular cartilage, leading to joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation. Each 5-unit increase in BMI has been associated with up to a 35% increased risk of knee OA. Moreover, obesity-related OA is not confined to weight-bearing joints; it is also seen in the hands, suggesting a systemic component to the disease pathogenesis. This systemic component involves chronic low-grade inflammation driven by adipokines (e.g., leptin, adiponectin, resistin) and proinflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) secreted by adipose tissue. These mediators disrupt cartilage homeostasis by increasing matrix metalloproteinase activity and decreasing the synthesis of type II collagen and aggrecan, leading to cartilage degradation. Additionally, obesity impairs muscle strength and joint stability, which exacerbates joint loading and hastens structural damage. Weight loss of as little as 10% has been shown to significantly reduce pain and improve function in patients with knee OA. Given these findings, weight reduction is considered a cornerstone of both primary and secondary OA prevention.

subject: MSK
stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department after injuring his right ankle during a recreational soccer match. While attempting to block a shot, he planted his right foot and twisted awkwardly, experiencing a sudden and forceful eversion of the ankle. He immediately felt severe pain and was unable to bear weight. On physical examination, the right ankle is swollen and bruised medially. Palpation along the medial malleolus elicits marked tenderness, and passive eversion of the foot reproduces the pain. The lateral aspect is non-tender, and the anterior drawer test is negative. Neurovascular status is intact. An x-ray of the right ankle is obtained and shown below. Which of the following structures is most likely injured?
explanation: This young man experienced a forcible eversion of the ankle while planting his foot, which is the classic mechanism of injury for deltoid ligament rupture, the major medial stabilizer of the ankle joint. The deltoid ligament originates from the medial malleolus and inserts into the navicular, talus, and calcaneus bones, resisting eversion of the foot. Clinical signs include medial ankle swelling, tenderness along the medial malleolus, and pain with eversion. These features are all present in this case. The x-ray further supports the diagnosis. There is an increased medial joint space, suggesting instability or widening due to ligamentous disruption. Although the deltoid ligament is strong and rarely injured in isolation, a sufficiently forceful eversion, such as in this high-impact sports setting, can result in a tear. Importantly, the lateral ligaments are typically spared, and there is no tenderness on the lateral malleolus in this case. Additionally, the anterior drawer test is negative, suggesting that the anterior talofibular ligament, the most commonly injured ligament in ankle sprains, is intact. The absence of findings such as lateral ecchymosis or tenderness rules out lateral ankle sprains. A medial injury with radiographic joint space widening confirms a deltoid ligament injury.

subject: MSK
stem: A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up of joint symptoms. Over the past few years, she has experienced intermittent pain and swelling in her hands, wrists, and knees. These symptoms tend to worsen in the mornings and improve slightly throughout the day. She has had several similar flares in the past that lasted a few weeks and resolved with over-the-counter medications. The patient reports increased fatigue and stiffness recently, especially in the mornings. She works part-time as a librarian and has difficulty shelving books during flares. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. She has a BMI of 32.1 kg/m² and smokes one pack of cigarettes per day. She drinks one glass of wine nightly and is not on any disease-modifying medications. Temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 142/90 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling in both wrists and the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody, with mild elevation in ESR and CRP. Which of the following interventions would most likely decrease this patient’s risk of recurrent disease flares?
explanation: Smoking is one of the most strongly established environmental risk factors for both the development and progression of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), particularly in genetically predisposed individuals. It is thought to enhance citrullination of proteins, stimulating autoantibody formation (e.g., anti-CCP), and promoting chronic synovial inflammation. In patients with existing RA, smoking is associated with increased disease activity, more frequent flares, and reduced response to therapy. Reducing or eliminating tobacco exposure has been shown to improve treatment outcomes, reduce flare frequency, and limit long-term joint damage. While pharmacologic therapy is essential for RA, addressing modifiable lifestyle risk factors, such as smoking, is critical in disease control. In this case, the patient’s continued tobacco use is likely contributing to recurrent flares and diminished control of symptoms. Advising smoking cessation is a nonpharmacologic strategy that can yield meaningful improvement in disease trajectory and response to disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs). The mainstay of RA management includes early initiation of DMARDs (e.g., methotrexate as first-line), short-term use of NSAIDs or glucocorticoids for symptom control, and adjunctive measures such as smoking cessation, weight loss, and physical therapy to optimize long-term outcomes.

subject: MSK
stem: A 70-year-old man comes to the outpatient clinic because of a bulge on the lower left side of his abdomen. He first noticed the swelling a few months ago while getting out of bed, and since then, it has gradually become more noticeable, particularly when standing or straining. He denies any trauma, prior abdominal surgery, or heavy lifting. He reports no abdominal pain, changes in bowel habits, nausea, or weight loss. His medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. On physical examination, there is a soft, reducible mass in the left lower quadrant of the abdominal wall, located several centimeters lateral to the lateral border of the rectus abdominis. The swelling increases with Valsalva maneuver and reduces with gentle pressure. There is no overlying erythema, tenderness, or signs of bowel obstruction. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with a reducible bulge in the lower lateral abdominal wall, lateral to the rectus abdominis, which is characteristic of a Spigelian hernia. Spigelian hernias are rare, comprising less than 2% of all abdominal wall hernias, but they are tested on USMLE. The hernia occurs at the semilunar line (linea semilunaris), where the aponeurosis of the transversus abdominis muscle transitions to become the lateral border of the rectus sheath. The most high-yield anatomical detail is that the medial border of the hernia is the rectus abdominis. These hernias tend to occur in older adults and are often overlooked clinically because the defect is in the muscular layer and not always obvious on inspection alone. However, in this vignette, the mass is reducible, exaggerated with Valsalva, and clearly lateral to the rectus muscle, which strongly supports the diagnosis. The absence of scrotal involvement, midline location, or prior surgical scars helps rule out more common hernias. Even though it is rare, the specific anatomical location and demographic of the patient make Spigelian hernia the correct answer here.

subject: MSK
stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to difficulty using her hands for the past several months. She reports struggling to grasp small objects such as keys and has recently needed assistance with tasks like buttoning her blouse and opening jars. She denies any recent trauma, falls, or fever. Her symptoms have been slowly progressive and worse on the right. She also describes intermittent tingling and dull aching pain that radiates down her right arm, sometimes reaching the hand. She does not report neck stiffness, gait instability, or bowel/bladder incontinence. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both controlled with medication. Neurologic examination shows decreased grip strength and reduced fine motor coordination on the right. Reflexes are symmetric, and no spasticity or hyperreflexia is observed. There is patchy sensory loss in the right hand. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with progressive weakness of hand muscles, impaired fine motor function, and radicular arm pain, particularly affecting distal function like grasping and manipulation. These findings point toward involvement of a lower cervical spinal nerve root that innervates the hand, specifically implicating a lesion at the level of the neural foramen. The symptoms are asymmetric, gradual in onset, and associated with sensory changes without signs of upper motor neuron involvement, suggesting a peripheral nerve root pathology rather than central spinal cord disease. Cervical foraminal stenosis is a condition in which the bony openings (foramina) through which cervical spinal nerves exit the spinal canal become narrowed, usually due to osteophytes, disc degeneration, or facet hypertrophy. This leads to nerve root compression. Involvement of lower cervical nerve roots (such as C8 or T1) can manifest with weakness in the intrinsic hand muscles and sensory changes in the hand or forearm. Unlike cervical spinal canal stenosis, which presents with signs of myelopathy such as gait instability, spasticity, and hyperreflexia, foraminal stenosis affects individual nerve roots and causes radiculopathy. This patient lacks signs of spinal cord involvement and instead has radicular symptoms localized to one upper limb, supporting the diagnosis of foraminal stenosis. An MRI of the cervical spine focused on the neural foramina would reveal the compressed exiting nerve root, and this imaging would guide further management, which may include conservative therapy or surgical decompression depending on severity.

subject: MSK
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty moving her right index finger after an accident at home earlier in the morning. She had been rearranging storage boxes on a high shelf in her garage when a plastic container unexpectedly fell. She reflexively reached out with her right hand to catch it, but the corner of the box struck the tip of her outstretched index finger with force. She felt a sharp pain immediately and has since been unable to use the fingertip normally. She denies numbness, tingling, or pain in the other fingers. On examination, the right hand appears normal with no swelling or bruising. There is no tenderness along the metacarpals or carpal bones. The index finger has full passive range of motion, and she can extend it fully. However, when asked to actively bend the fingertip of the index finger, she is unable to flex the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint, while proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal motion remains intact. The rest of the hand functions normally. Which of the following tendons is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: This 42-year-old woman presents with isolated loss of flexion at the distal interphalangeal joint of the index finger following direct trauma to the fingertip, which occurred when a falling box struck the finger in extension. This presentation is highly suggestive of injury to the flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) tendon, the only tendon responsible for flexion at the DIP joint of the fingers. The mechanism — sudden extension force applied to a flexed fingertip — is classic for a jersey finger-type injury, even though this case is not sports-related. The FDP originates from the proximal ulna and interosseous membrane, runs along the volar forearm, and inserts on the base of the distal phalanges of digits 2–5. Injury to this tendon presents with isolated loss of DIP flexion, while the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) and metacarpophalangeal (MTP) joints remain unaffected. This patient's inability to flex only the tip of the index finger, with preserved function elsewhere, makes the diagnosis clear. There are no signs of bony deformity or neurovascular injury, further supporting a clean, isolated FDP lesion. The FDP flexes the DIP joints, while the flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) flexes the PIP joints. A key diagnostic clue is that PIP flexion remains intact in this case, ruling out FDS injury.

subject: MSK
stem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling from a tree onto his outstretched right hand. He reports severe pain in the elbow and forearm, along with swelling that developed rapidly after the fall. He has difficulty moving his fingers due to pain. There is no obvious deformity of the forearm or hand, but the elbow appears swollen and tender. Passive extension of the fingers worsens his forearm pain. Radial pulse on the injured side is faint compared to the opposite arm. The fingers are warm but capillary refill is delayed. Sensory testing shows intact sensation over the thenar eminence and dorsal thumb. Radiographs of the elbow are ordered and demonstrate a displaced fracture. Injury to which of the following anatomic locations is most likely responsible for this patient’s vascular compromise?

options.A: Distal third of the humeral shaft

explanation: This patient has findings consistent with a supracondylar fracture of the humerus, a common pediatric elbow injury that often occurs after a fall onto an outstretched hand (FOOSH). The mechanism transmits force up the forearm to the distal humerus, where the thin bone between the medial and lateral columns is prone to fracture just above the condyles. In children aged 5–10, the supracondylar region is the weakest point of the distal humerus. The most important complication of supracondylar fractures is neurovascular injury, especially to the brachial artery and median nerve. The brachial artery runs anterior to the humerus and is tethered proximally at the axilla and distally at the elbow joint, making it vulnerable to stretching or transection when the distal fracture fragment is displaced. Posterior displacement of the distal fragment can compress or tear the artery, leading to absent or diminished distal pulses, prolonged capillary refill, and signs of forearm ischemia. Median nerve injury may cause sensory deficits in the lateral palm and motor deficits affecting thumb opposition and flexion. If arterial injury is not recognized and repaired promptly, compartment syndrome can develop from ischemia–reperfusion injury. The clinical presentation here—fall onto outstretched hand, swelling around the elbow, weak radial pulse, delayed capillary refill, and severe pain with passive finger extension—is highly suggestive of this injury pattern. Warm fingers indicate some collateral flow through the radial recurrent or ulnar arteries, but the delayed refill shows compromised primary flow through the brachial artery. Management includes urgent orthopedic evaluation, immobilization, and reduction to restore alignment and vascular flow. If the pulse remains absent or markedly reduced after reduction, surgical exploration and repair of the brachial artery may be required. Early intervention prevents long-term ischemic complications and preserves limb function.

subject: MSK
stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that has progressively worsened over the past month. He says the pain began after he tripped during basketball practice, but there was no fall or direct trauma. Since then, he has been avoiding sports and complains of intermittent pain that radiates to the thigh. His mother reports that he has been less active recently and appears to be limping, especially at the end of the day. Past medical history is notable for childhood asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids, and a learning disability for which he attends special classes. He was born at term by cesarean section due to breech presentation. His diet consists largely of processed foods, and he spends several hours daily playing video games. On examination, his BMI is at the 98th percentile. He walks with an antalgic gait, and there is limited internal rotation of the right hip. Knee examination is unremarkable, and there is no joint effusion or erythema. Passive flexion of the hip elicits discomfort. Neurologic exam is normal. Which of the following is the most significant risk factor for this patient’s condition?

options.C: History of asthma treated with corticosteroids

explanation: This patient has slipped capital femoral epiphysis, suggested by adolescent age, obesity, antalgic gait, referred knee pain, and limited hip internal rotation with an otherwise normal knee exam. High BMI is the strongest risk factor because excess mechanical stress across the proximal femoral physis predisposes the femoral head epiphysis to slip relative to the neck. Breech presentation is a risk factor for developmental dysplasia of the hip, not SCFE.

subject: MSK
stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the outpatient clinic due to pain in her right heel that has been bothering her for the past two months. She says the pain is most noticeable when she first gets out of bed in the morning. It feels like a sharp pulling sensation along the bottom of her heel, which sometimes radiates forward along the arch of her foot. After walking for a few minutes, the pain lessens, and she is generally able to go about her day with only mild discomfort. However, the pain often returns if she has been sitting or inactive for long periods and then stands up again. She has not had any recent trauma or systemic symptoms. She works in retail and spends most of her day standing or walking on hard floors. Her BMI is 31 kg/m². On physical examination, there is point tenderness at the medial calcaneal tuberosity of the right foot. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with heel pain that is worst when first getting out of bed in the morning, which is the classic presentation of plantar fasciitis, a degenerative condition involving the origin of the plantar fascia on the calcaneus. The sharp pain described on first steps after rest and improvement with continued walking is nearly pathognomonic and HY on USMLE. The pain also tends to recur after prolonged periods of sitting or inactivity, a hallmark of this condition. She has no systemic symptoms, trauma, or visible inflammation, which helps rule out other causes. Plantar fasciitis typically affects middle-aged individuals and is more common in those with occupations requiring prolonged standing or walking, as well as in patients with obesity or poor footwear support. The plantar fascia connects the calcaneus to the toes and functions as a shock absorber and arch support. Repeated microtrauma to its insertion site leads to localized inflammation and degenerative changes. The medial calcaneal tuberosity is the most common site of pain and maximal tenderness. In this vignette, the physical exam confirms point tenderness at the medial heel, with no swelling or erythema, further supporting a localized soft tissue cause. The morning pain pattern and improvement with walking are distinctive. There is no need for imaging in a straightforward case; diagnosis is clinical. Treatment includes stretching exercises, footwear modification, and activity adjustments.

subject: MSK
stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a painful limp that has worsened over the past three weeks. He reports pain localized to his left groin and knee, worsened with activity. His BMI is 23 kg/m², and he is otherwise healthy with no history of trauma. Physical examination reveals limited internal rotation and abduction of the left hip. When standing, his left leg appears externally rotated and shorter than the right. Passive range of motion elicits discomfort at the left hip. His x-ray findings are shown below. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: SCFE results from displacement of the femoral epiphysis relative to the femoral neck at the growth plate and typically affects preadolescent children aged 10–13 years. The condition is more common in boys and often presents as progressive hip, groin, or knee pain with a limp. Physical exam findings include decreased internal rotation and abduction, leg shortening, and external rotation. X-ray typically shows posterior and inferior displacement of the femoral head on the neck (“ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance). Obesity is a risk factor, but a normal BMI child can still present with SCFE. The pathology involves mechanical failure of the hypertrophic zone of the growth plate during periods of rapid growth. Left-sided involvement can cause referred knee pain despite hip pathology due to shared innervation. Early recognition is essential because untreated SCFE may lead to avascular necrosis and early osteoarthritis. The primary treatment is surgical pinning in situ to stabilize the femoral head and prevent progression.

subject: MSK
stem: A 67-year-old man comes to the clinic because of gradually worsening discomfort in both shoulders and elbows over the past 2 years. The pain is worse in the evenings after his work as a handyman and improves somewhat with rest. He reports mild stiffness in the morning that lasts only a few minutes before improving. He denies fevers, rash, weight loss, or recent infections. There is no history of joint trauma or inflammatory arthritis. His medical history includes controlled hypertension. Examination shows crepitus with passive movement of the shoulders and elbows, without warmth or significant swelling. There is mild limitation in the range of motion due to discomfort. Strength is preserved, and sensation is intact. No nodules or skin lesions are present. Laboratory studies, including ESR and CRP, are within normal limits. The involved joint is most likely to show which of the following?

options.B: Chondrocyte necrosis with calcium pyrophosphate deposition
options.C: Loss of proteoglycan matrix with uric acid crystal deposition
options.D: Subarticular bone plate exposure and hyperactive bone formation
options.E: Synovial hyperplasia with lymphoid follicle formation

answer_text: Subarticular bone plate exposure and hyperactive bone formation
explanation: This patient’s presentation of chronic, progressively worsening joint pain involving large joints with brief morning stiffness, crepitus, and normal inflammatory markers is consistent with osteoarthritis (OA). In OA, the degenerative process leads to progressive loss of articular cartilage, eventually exposing the subarticular (subchondral) bone plate. In response to altered joint mechanics and increased stress, there is compensatory hyperactive bone formation, leading to sclerosis and osteophyte development, visible radiographically. These findings match the most likely structural change in the affected joints for this patient. OA is a noninflammatory degenerative joint disease characterized by mechanical wear and tear of articular cartilage. Repetitive use and aging lead to biochemical changes, including decreased synthesis of type II collagen and proteoglycans, impairing the cartilage’s ability to resist compressive forces. Cartilage fibrillation progresses to complete erosion in focal areas, exposing the subchondral bone plate. This exposed bone becomes sclerotic due to increased osteoblastic activity, and marginal osteophytes form in an attempt to stabilize the joint. OA is most often caused by chronic mechanical stress from aging, repetitive joint use, or prior injury, but secondary OA can result from metabolic disorders (eg, hemochromatosis, ochronosis), joint malalignment, or chronic inflammatory arthritis. Risk factors include advanced age, female sex, obesity, prior joint trauma, and occupations requiring repetitive motion. Joints most commonly affected include the knees, hips, cervical and lumbar spine (facet joints), first carpometacarpal joint of the thumb, and the distal (Heberden nodes) and proximal (Bouchard nodes) interphalangeal joints. Clinical features of OA may also include joint instability, reduced joint space–related deformities, locking or catching of the joint, and decreased functional capacity. Pain often worsens with prolonged activity and improves with rest. Diagnosis is clinical, supported by imaging that shows joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, cyst formation, and osteophytes. Laboratory testing is usually unremarkable, which helps exclude inflammatory causes of arthritis. Management begins with nonpharmacologic strategies such as weight loss, activity modification, and physical therapy to improve joint mechanics and strengthen surrounding musculature. Pharmacologic therapy includes acetaminophen for mild symptoms and NSAIDs for more severe or refractory pain; topical NSAIDs and capsaicin can be used for localized joint involvement. Intra-articular corticosteroid injections may provide temporary relief for acute exacerbations. Viscosupplementation (hyaluronic acid injections) may be considered in selected patients. Surgical interventions, including arthroscopic debridement or joint replacement (total arthroplasty), are indicated for advanced disease with significant pain and functional limitation despite maximal medical therapy.
bottom_line: Subarticular bone plate exposure and hyperactive bone formation

subject: MSK
stem: A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressively worsening bilateral knee pain that began over three years ago. She describes the pain as dull and aching, most noticeable after walking or prolonged standing. She denies morning stiffness, systemic symptoms, or joint redness. Her past medical history includes obesity and venous stasis. On exam, she has crepitus with passive knee motion and visible bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal joints. No erythema or warmth is present. Her BMI is 42 kg/m². Synovial fluid aspirated from the right knee is clear with a WBC count of 950/μL and normal glucose levels. A plain radiograph shows asymmetric joint space narrowing and evidence of subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition? Immune complex deposition with complement activation and neutrophil recruitment Chondrocyte-mediated cartilage breakdown and subchondral bone eburnation Autoimmune pannus formation causing destruction of articular cartilage

options.A: Synovial seeding by hematogenous bacteria leading to purulent inflammation
options.B: Calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition within hyaline cartilage
options.C: Chondrocyte-mediated cartilage breakdown and subchondral bone eburnation
options.D: Immune complex deposition with complement activation and neutrophil recruitment
options.E: Autoimmune pannus formation causing destruction of articular cartilage

answer_text: Chondrocyte-mediated cartilage breakdown and subchondral bone eburnation
explanation: This presentation is classic for osteoarthritis: chronic activity-related knee pain, crepitus, obesity, Heberden nodes, low-cell-count noninflammatory synovial fluid, and radiographic joint-space narrowing with subchondral sclerosis. The underlying process is chondrocyte-mediated breakdown of articular cartilage followed by exposure and remodeling of subchondral bone (eburnation) and osteophyte formation. This is a degenerative, not inflammatory, arthropathy.
bottom_line: Chondrocyte-mediated cartilage breakdown and subchondral bone eburnation

subject: MSK
stem: A 23-year-old man comes to the clinic with left knee pain that started during a recreational soccer match two days ago. While attempting to block a shot, another player struck the outside of his planted left knee, causing it to buckle inward. He immediately felt pain in the knee and had to stop playing. Since then, the pain has persisted, especially with turning or lateral movement, but he can bear weight. On physical exam, there is diffuse mild swelling and tenderness of the knee. When inward pressure is applied to the lateral side of the knee while stabilizing the medial ankle, the joint opens more medially than the opposite side. There is no visible deformity, joint locking, or gross instability. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with medial knee pain after sustaining a lateral blow to the knee, resulting in inward buckling — the classic mechanism of medial collateral ligament (MCL) injury. The MCL stabilizes the medial aspect of the knee joint and resists valgus stress. When compromised, pressure applied from lateral to medial causes the knee to open excessively along the medial side, especially compared to the contralateral leg. The physical exam description — with inward force applied to the lateral knee and resulting increased medial movement — corresponds to a positive valgus stress test. Patients can often have localized medial tenderness, joint line pain, and swelling (although in this patient we say more diffuse). The ability to bear weight and absence of mechanical symptoms suggest a low- to moderate-grade MCL sprain, rather than a more complex intra-articular injury. MRI may be used to confirm the diagnosis or assess for concomitant ligament injuries in more severe trauma. Management of isolated MCL injury depends on severity but is typically nonoperative. Initial treatment includes rest, ice, compression, and elevation (RICE), along with NSAIDs and temporary activity modification. A hinged knee brace is often used to support stability while the ligament heals. Most patients recover with physical therapy focused on restoring range of motion and strengthening surrounding musculature. Surgical repair is rarely needed unless there is complete rupture or multiligament involvement.

subject: MSK
stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the clinic after noticing a bump on the back of his right wrist that he first saw two weeks ago. He works in IT support and spends most of the day typing or using a mouse. The lesion has gradually increased in size, but he denies any associated pain, numbness, weakness, redness, or warmth. He is concerned it may be a tumor. He has no prior history of joint pain, trauma, or systemic illness. He has not tried any medications or therapies. On physical examination, a 2-cm round swelling is observed on the dorsal aspect of the right wrist, as shown in the image below. The mass is soft, non-tender, and mobile under the skin. There is no discoloration or warmth over the area. Wrist range of motion is full and symmetric, and grip strength is preserved. No skin lesions or other joint abnormalities are seen. The patient has no constitutional symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Giant cell tumor of tendon sheath

explanation: This patient presents with a classic dorsal wrist mass that is painless, round, mobile, and slowly enlarging. These features, along with the absence of constitutional or inflammatory signs and the lesion's location on the dorsum of the wrist, are highly suggestive of a ganglion cyst. These cysts arise from herniation of joint connective tissue, resulting in a gelatinous collection of synovial fluid. They are commonly found over the scapholunate joint, which is the most frequent site for ganglion formation. Importantly, the lesion is not causing functional limitation, pain, or neurologic compromise. In such cases, the recommended approach is observation, as the natural history of ganglion cysts is often benign. In fact, many regress spontaneously without intervention. While patients may request aspiration or removal for cosmetic or symptomatic reasons, recurrence is common with both needle drainage and surgical excision. Intervention is generally reserved for discomfort or impairment, neither of which is present here. The image confirms a smooth, subcutaneous swelling over the dorsal wrist with no signs of inflammation, making alternative diagnoses unlikely.

subject: MSK
stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after falling off a motorcycle at high speed. He was wearing a helmet and did not lose consciousness. On examination, he has multiple abrasions and a deep laceration over the posterior mid-arm. He is placed in a sling and reports difficulty extending his wrist. Physical exam reveals wrist and finger extension weakness as well as diminished sensation over the dorsum of the right hand and posterior forearm. Elbow flexion and shoulder abduction are preserved. Reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?
explanation: Radial nerve injury is a classic complication of midshaft humeral fractures or deep lacerations to the posterior arm. The radial nerve traverses the radial (spiral) groove of the humerus and is responsible for innervating the extensor muscles of the wrist and fingers, as well as the skin over the posterior forearm and dorsum of the hand. Damage at this level causes wrist drop and impaired finger extension, along with sensory loss over the radial distribution. Because the triceps branches off more proximally, triceps reflex and elbow extension are typically preserved when the injury occurs at the mid-humerus. This explains why this patient has motor and sensory deficits without reflex loss. Recognizing the nerve injured based on trauma location and pattern of sensory/motor loss is key. A posterior humeral shaft wound or fracture should immediately raise suspicion for radial nerve damage. The physical signs—wrist drop and dorsal hand numbness—correspond to this nerve’s distribution. Diagnosis is clinical, and management includes wound care, splinting, and follow-up for nerve recovery. Surgical intervention is considered if function fails to improve over time.

subject: MSK
stem: A 58-year-old man comes to the neurology clinic with several months of worsening weakness in his dominant right hand. He works as a carpenter and has noticed increasing difficulty using his tools, particularly when trying to hold small nails between his fingers or when stabilizing pieces of wood that require hand dexterity. He frequently drops screws or washers, especially when trying to pick them up with his ring and little fingers. Over time, he has also observed a slow but progressive shrinkage of the muscles between his fingers and around the base of his little finger. He initially thought it was due to aging and overuse, but now it is beginning to interfere with his work and daily functioning. He denies pain, trauma, or systemic symptoms but does report intermittent numbness and tingling in the medial hand that worsens with prolonged elbow flexion or when resting his arm on a hard surface. He has a past medical history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He does not smoke and drinks socially. He sleeps with his arms bent under his pillow, and he sometimes notices hand numbness in the morning that resolves after a few minutes. At work, he frequently leans his elbows against counters and tool benches. He has tried wearing a wrist splint at night without significant benefit. He has not seen any change in his grip strength while shaking hands or squeezing with his palm but finds tasks requiring finger spreading or pinching between fingers increasingly difficult. He also notes trouble playing guitar, especially forming chords that require abducting and adducting individual fingers. He has no symptoms in his left hand and no known history of neck problems. On physical exam, there is marked wasting of the hypothenar eminence and the first dorsal interosseous muscle on the right. The hand shows mild clawing of the fourth and fifth digits at rest. Strength testing reveals weakness in finger abduction and adduction, most noticeable when asked to spread his fingers against resistance or hold a piece of paper between the fingers. Sensation is reduced over the palmar and dorsal surfaces of the fifth digit and the medial half of the fourth digit. Grip strength is mildly reduced but wrist flexion, elbow strength, and forearm pronation/supination are intact. Tinel sign is positive at the right elbow. The physician explains that the pattern of sensory and motor findings indicates damage to a specific nerve supplying most of the intrinsic hand muscles. Which of the following nerves is primarily responsible for this patient’s deficits?
explanation: The ulnar nerve is responsible for innervating the interosseous muscles of the hand, which control finger abduction and adduction. In this patient, the inability to spread the fingers or hold paper between them—known as Froment’s sign when the adductor pollicis is involved—is a hallmark of ulnar neuropathy, particularly involving the deep motor branch. Additionally, atrophy of the first dorsal interosseous muscle and hypothenar wasting further localize the lesion to the ulnar nerve. These findings are especially pronounced in cubital tunnel syndrome, where compression occurs at the elbow, frequently due to external pressure or repetitive elbow flexion. The ulnar nerve passes through the cubital tunnel, a fibro-osseous canal at the medial elbow, where it is susceptible to compression. The interossei include the dorsal interossei, which abduct the fingers (DAB), and the palmar interossei, which adduct the fingers (PAD). These are crucial for fine motor control, such as playing musical instruments or manipulating small tools, as described in this patient. In addition to these muscles, the ulnar nerve supplies the medial two lumbricals, the adductor pollicis, and the hypothenar muscles. The sensory innervation by the ulnar nerve includes the palmar and dorsal medial hand, especially the fifth digit and medial half of the fourth digit, which matches this patient’s sensory loss. The presence of claw hand deformity is due to unopposed action of the long extensors and loss of lumbrical function, particularly affecting the fourth and fifth digits. Chronic ulnar neuropathy may result from cubital tunnel syndrome, Guyon’s canal syndrome, or systemic conditions like diabetes. The most common site of compression is the elbow, especially in people who lean on hard surfaces or sleep with elbows flexed, as in this patient. Early identification and conservative treatment such as elbow splinting, avoiding repetitive stress, and physical therapy may help. However, in advanced cases with muscle wasting and persistent weakness, surgical decompression may be necessary to prevent permanent motor loss. Understanding the ulnar nerve’s motor distribution, especially to the interossei, is crucial in diagnosing and managing hand weakness.

subject: MSK
stem: A 48-year-old man comes to the emergency department with a swollen, painful left leg. He says this happened once before two years ago and was told he had a “blood clot.” He recently returned from a 9-hour international flight and has gained about 25 lbs over the past year. He works a desk job and spends most of his day sitting. He also takes a daily selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor for depression and has a BMI of 33 kg/m². Vital signs are: temperature 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure 128/84 mmHg, heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. On examination, his left leg is warm, erythematous, and tender to palpation. Duplex ultrasonography confirms the presence of a deep vein thrombosis. Which of the following factors in this patient’s history most increased his risk for developing this condition?
explanation: A prior history of DVT is one of the strongest predictors of recurrence. Patients who have had symptomatic DVT in the past have a 17–30% risk of developing another episode, especially if the initial cause was unprovoked or incompletely treated. Recurrence risk is highest within the first few years after the initial event, but persists lifelong. Therefore, when evaluating a patient with a history of thrombosis and new symptoms, clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion and consider prior DVT the most significant risk factor among others. DVT classically presents with unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, and tenderness—though it may be asymptomatic in some cases. If left untreated, up to 50% of proximal DVTs may result in pulmonary embolism, which carries a significant risk of morbidity and mortality. DVT development is rooted in Virchow’s triad: stasis (eg, long flight, postoperative immobility), hypercoagulability (eg, oral contraceptive use, hypercoagulable disorders), and endothelial damage (eg, smoking, hypertension, atherosclerosis, injury). Initial management of confirmed DVT includes prompt initiation of anticoagulation therapy with agents such as low molecular weight heparin, unfractionated heparin, or direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) like rivaroxaban or apixaban. In patients with high bleeding risk, mechanical interventions such as IVC filters may be considered.

subject: MSK
stem: A 21-year-old college football player presents to the clinic with right knee pain that started during a game two days ago. He was running toward the sideline when an opposing player collided with the inside of his right knee while his foot was planted. He immediately felt pain along the outside of his knee and had difficulty continuing to play. The pain has persisted since the injury, especially during walking or lateral movement. On physical examination, there is mild swelling and localized tenderness over the lateral aspect of the right knee. When the examiner applies gentle pressure outward on the medial side of the knee while stabilizing the lateral ankle, the right knee exhibits more lateral movement than the left. There is no bruising, deformity, or joint locking. Passive range of motion is preserved. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This young athlete presents with lateral knee pain following a direct blow to the medial side of the knee during a contact sport, with exam findings showing increased lateral joint movement when stress is applied. This mechanism and physical exam are classic for injury to the lateral collateral ligament (LCL). The LCL functions to stabilize the knee against varus stress — that is, forces that push the knee outward relative to the femur. When injured, application of a medial-to-lateral force on the knee (a varus test) reveals excessive lateral motion compared to the uninjured side. The patient’s symptoms — pain along the lateral knee, instability during lateral movement, and localized tenderness — support involvement of the LCL. While mild swelling can occur, the absence of locking or mechanical symptoms helps exclude meniscal or intra-articular injury. MRI is the confirmatory test of choice. Treatment depends on the severity of the injury. Isolated, low-grade LCL sprains can often be managed conservatively with rest, ice, compression, elevation (RICE), bracing, and physical therapy. High-grade tears, particularly those involving posterolateral corner structures or other ligaments, may require surgical repair or reconstruction.

subject: MSK
stem: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent discomfort in his right thigh and groin that has worsened over the past 3 weeks. He first noticed a dull ache after participating in a weekend soccer game, which he attributed to overexertion. The pain has not resolved with rest or over-the-counter analgesics, and he has been avoiding physical activity at school. His mother has noticed a progressive limp, particularly when he climbs stairs. He also mentions feeling more tired than usual and says he sometimes feels stiffness in the mornings, although this usually improves within an hour. His past medical history includes childhood eczema and mild asthma treated with inhaled corticosteroids. His diet is low in calcium-rich foods, and he spends most of his time indoors playing video games. There is no history of trauma, recent travel, rashes, or fever. His BMI is at the 97th percentile, and he is otherwise developmentally normal. On exam, he has a slightly externally rotated right lower extremity while standing. Gait assessment reveals limping and guarding on the right side. There is decreased range of motion at the right hip, especially with internal rotation. Knee and lumbar spine exams are unremarkable. There is no joint effusion or erythema. Neurologic exam is intact. A radiograph of the pelvis is shown below. Which of the following best describes the pathologic mechanism of this patient’s condition?

options.A: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
options.B: Contracture of the capital femoral epiphysis
options.C: Displacement of the epiphysis of the femoral head
options.D: Fracture through the proximal femoral growth plate
options.E: Inflammatory destruction of the femoral metaphysis

answer_text: Displacement of the epiphysis of the femoral head
explanation: This patient has slipped capital femoral epiphysis, and the pathologic mechanism is displacement of the femoral head epiphysis relative to the femoral neck through the growth plate. Obesity, limp, externally rotated leg, and decreased hip internal rotation are classic clues. This differs from Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease, which is due to avascular necrosis rather than physeal slippage.
bottom_line: Displacement of the epiphysis of the femoral head

subject: MSK
stem: A 16-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to persistent, intermittent left-sided chest pain for the past 4 days. The pain is described as sharp, non-radiating, and comes on suddenly at rest. He denies any cough, fever, palpitations, or shortness of breath. He reports that the pain does not change with food intake, but it worsens with certain upper body movements, including lifting his backpack or swinging a bat. He plays competitive baseball and had an intense upper body workout earlier this week. There is no history of recent viral illness or trauma. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. On physical examination, he appears well and not in distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations are unremarkable. Palpation of the anterior chest wall reveals point tenderness over the left 2nd and 3rd costosternal junctions without swelling, erythema, or bruising. ECG and chest X-ray performed in the emergency department two days ago were normal. Laboratory tests including troponin, CBC, and CRP are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Costochondritis is an inflammatory condition affecting the costosternal junctions, most commonly seen in adolescents and young adults. It presents with localized anterior chest pain that is sharp, non-radiating, and worsens with certain movements or palpation. It does not involve swelling and is usually self-limiting. The hallmark is reproducible pain over the costochondral or costosternal junctions. The condition often arises after repeated physical strain or minor trauma, such as vigorous upper body exercise, weightlifting, or repetitive movements in sports like baseball. There is typically no associated systemic illness, fever, or respiratory symptoms. Risk factors include poor posture, overuse injuries, and sometimes recent upper respiratory infections, though this patient lacks such signs. On physical exam, the key feature is tenderness to palpation over the anterior chest wall, specifically at the costosternal or costochondral junctions. There are no signs of erythema, swelling, or systemic inflammation. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal, and imaging is usually unremarkable. ECG, troponins, and inflammatory markers are not needed unless other etiologies are being considered, but when done, they are normal. Management is conservative. The mainstays of treatment are NSAIDs and activity modification. Most cases resolve within days to weeks. Educating the patient about the benign nature of the condition is essential to reduce anxiety. Physical therapy may help in chronic or recurrent cases. Importantly, serious causes like cardiac or pulmonary disease must first be ruled out if clinically indicated, especially in atypical presentations.

subject: MSK
stem: A 33-year-old construction worker comes to the clinic due to pain in his left knee that started 2 weeks ago after he pivoted awkwardly while stepping off scaffolding. He says the pain is located on the inner side of the knee and occasionally feels like something is "stuck" when he tries to fully straighten it. He reports a few instances of a clicking sensation during extension while transitioning from a squatting to a standing position. He denies instability or swelling, but avoids kneeling or crouching due to discomfort. On examination, there is localized tenderness along the medial joint line, and when the leg is externally rotated during extension from a flexed position, he winces and describes a catching sensation. There is no visible effusion, and the remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with medial knee pain, mechanical symptoms of catching and locking, and pain with external rotation during knee extension, all of which are classic for a medial meniscal tear. The medial meniscus is more tightly attached to the joint capsule and is more frequently injured than the lateral meniscus. Twisting or pivoting movements, especially with a planted foot and flexed knee, are common mechanisms of injury in active individuals. Meniscal tears result in mechanical obstruction of motion within the joint. Patients typically report pain, locking, catching, or a clicking sensation with movement. The exam maneuver described — the McMurray test, which involves external rotation of the leg during extension — reproduces pain and clicking along the medial joint line and is highly suggestive of a medial meniscus injury. Although swelling may be absent in subacute or chronic cases, medial joint line tenderness and a positive McMurray test are valuable diagnostic clues. The patient’s functional limitation and avoidance of squatting are additional hints. The absence of instability, swelling, or patellar tracking issues helps to exclude ligamentous or anterior pathologies. MRI is the diagnostic test of choice and can guide treatment decisions. Small tears may respond to conservative management, while persistent mechanical symptoms or large tears may necessitate arthroscopic repair.

subject: MSK
stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with concerns about knee pain and stiffness that have developed gradually over the past 6 weeks. She describes aching in both knees, especially after a long day of teaching, and notes that the pain is worse when going up or down stairs. The stiffness tends to occur after prolonged sitting and improves somewhat with movement. She reports no redness, warmth, or swelling in the joints. Over the past year, she has gained about 9 kg (20 lb) due to emotional eating after her father’s passing. She admits to minimal exercise lately but had recently attempted to rejoin a weekend hiking group. After a few hikes, she found that her knee pain significantly worsened, which discouraged her from continuing. She has no history of joint disease, but her mother underwent knee replacement surgery in her 60s. She smokes about half a pack of cigarettes daily and has done so for nearly two decades. Her diet is irregular, and she often eats takeout or processed foods. She has no morning stiffness, no involvement of her hands or other joints, and no history of inflammatory disease. She takes no medications and has no allergies. On exam, her height is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) and weight is 91 kg (200 lb), giving her a BMI of 32.2 kg/m². Knee examination shows full range of motion, no effusion, and mild crepitus during flexion and extension. No tenderness is present over the joint lines or ligaments, and there is no erythema. Strength, sensation, and reflexes are intact in both lower extremities. The patient asks how she can reduce her risk of long-term joint problems. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.B: Daily use of a calcium supplement

explanation: Weight loss is the most effective intervention to reduce the risk of developing and worsening osteoarthritis in overweight or obese patients. Osteoarthritis is primarily a mechanical degenerative process that targets weight-bearing joints, and excess body weight substantially increases the load across the knees with every step. In fact, for every 1 kg (2.2 lb) of body weight lost, the load on the knee joint can be reduced by approximately 4 kg during daily activity. This patient's BMI over 30 kg/m² places her at significantly elevated risk for knee osteoarthritis. Weight reduction not only lessens joint stress but also lowers systemic inflammation, which may further reduce symptom burden and disease progression. Numerous studies have shown that weight loss improves pain, function, and radiographic progression in patients with osteoarthritis. This patient is already experiencing early mechanical symptoms of knee joint overload, including pain with stair use and stiffness after inactivity, but she lacks features of inflammatory arthritis. While her family history and age may predispose her to osteoarthritis, the most immediate and modifiable contributor in her case is obesity. Comprehensive management should involve caloric restriction, a structured low-impact exercise program (such as swimming or cycling), and patient education. Smoking cessation and improved diet are beneficial for general health, but they are not the most specific or effective recommendations for lowering this patient's osteoarthritis risk.

subject: MSK
stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to a persistent limp and decreased activity over the past 6 weeks. His parents note that he used to be very active but now avoids running and complains of stiffness in the morning. There is no fever, rash, trauma, or recent illness. The child denies night sweats or weight loss. He is of normal weight and height and has met all developmental milestones. On physical exam, internal rotation and abduction of the right hip elicit discomfort. Gait assessment reveals mild antalgic walking and subtle pelvic drop on the left when standing on the right leg. There is no joint effusion, warmth, or swelling noted. A pelvic radiograph is obtained and shown below. Which of the following best explains this patient’s current condition?

options.A: Abnormal development of the acetabulum leading to hip instability and subluxation
options.B: Chronic autoimmune inflammation of the synovium causing progressive joint erosion
options.C: Idiopathic disruption of blood flow to the femoral capital epiphysis leading to necrosis
options.D: Mechanical failure of the growth plate resulting in posterior epiphyseal displacement
options.E: Post-viral inflammation of the hip joint causing transient joint effusion and pain

answer_text: Idiopathic disruption of blood flow to the femoral capital epiphysis leading to necrosis
explanation: This 7-year-old with limp, limited hip abduction/internal rotation, Trendelenburg-type pelvic drop, and compatible radiographic findings most likely has Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease. The underlying mechanism is idiopathic interruption of blood supply to the capital femoral epiphysis, causing avascular necrosis of the femoral head. This differs from SCFE, which is a growth-plate slippage disorder seen more often in overweight adolescents.
bottom_line: Idiopathic disruption of blood flow to the femoral capital epiphysis leading to necrosis

subject: MSK
stem: A 26-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic for evaluation of a swelling on the back of his right wrist, which he noticed about a month ago. He says it has remained painless and has not changed in size over the past two weeks. He works as a graphic designer and frequently uses a keyboard and mouse but denies any recent trauma, weakness, or numbness. He has no history of systemic disease, fever, or weight loss. He states that the mass is sometimes more prominent at the end of the day but does not interfere with daily activities. On physical examination, the wrist is non-tender with full active and passive range of motion. A 2-cm, soft, well-circumscribed swelling is noted on the dorsal aspect of the wrist, as seen in the image below. The mass is freely mobile under the skin and does not elicit pain on palpation. There is no overlying erythema, warmth, or fluctuation. Neurologic exam of the upper extremities is normal. Which of the following is the most likely outcome if this condition is left untreated?

options.A: Bony erosion of underlying carpal bones
options.B: Complete resolution of the lesion over time
options.D: Post-viral inflammation of the hip joint causing transient joint effusion and pain
options.E: Malignant transformation into soft tissue sarcoma

answer_text: Complete resolution of the lesion over time
explanation: This patient presents with a painless, mobile, well-circumscribed swelling on the dorsal wrist with no signs of inflammation, systemic illness, or neurological compromise. These features are characteristic of a ganglion cyst, a common benign lesion caused by the accumulation of gelatinous synovial fluid from adjacent joint or tendon sheaths. The most frequent location is the dorsum of the wrist, particularly over the scapholunate ligament. Ganglion cysts are typically asymptomatic and discovered incidentally, as in this case. Because they are benign and not associated with aggressive or destructive behavior, the recommended management is conservative observation. There is no need for imaging or surgical referral unless the cyst causes discomfort, functional limitation, or cosmetic concern. While some patients may request needle aspiration or excision, these are not first-line in asymptomatic cases. Importantly, a significant proportion of ganglion cysts undergo spontaneous regression, especially in patients who avoid repetitive wrist strain. The lesion described in the image and vignette fits this classic presentation with no red flags for complications. The most likely outcome in this patient if no intervention is performed is complete spontaneous resolution. Continued reassurance and monitoring is the correct next step unless symptoms arise.
bottom_line: Complete resolution of the lesion over time

subject: MSK
stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with difficulty moving her right arm behind her back and discomfort in her shoulder for the past several weeks. She says the pain started gradually and is most noticeable when reaching to fasten her bra or retrieve items from her back pocket. She denies trauma, numbness, tingling, or prior shoulder surgery. The pain is dull, localized to the anterior shoulder, and not aggravated by overhead activity. It does not interfere with sleep. On physical examination, there is no swelling or asymmetry. Active range of motion is full in flexion and abduction. When asked to place the back of her hand against her lower back with her palm facing outward, she is unable to lift the hand away from her back. The movement reproduces her discomfort. The remainder of the shoulder exam is unremarkable. Which of the following muscles is most likely injured?
explanation: This 40-year-old woman presents with progressive difficulty reaching behind her back and reproducing shoulder pain during internal rotation. The physical exam describes the Gerber lift-off test, where the patient places the dorsum of her hand on the lower back with the palm facing outward and attempts to lift it away from the body. Inability to do so —especially if painful—is a specific test for subscapularis pathology, which is responsible for medial rotation and adduction of the arm. The fact that abduction and flexion are preserved helps localize the weakness specifically to internal rotators. The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that performs internal (medial) rotation of the shoulder and also assists in adduction. It is innervated by the upper and lower subscapular nerves, both branches of the posterior cord of the brachial plexus. Injury or tendinopathy of the subscapularis may occur with chronic overuse or subtle degenerative change, particularly in middle-aged patients who repetitively reach behind the back or perform medial rotation. Pain or weakness elicited by the lift-off test strongly supports involvement of this muscle. Importantly, other rotator cuff muscles such as the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor are primarily responsible for abduction or external rotation, not internal movement.

subject: MSK
stem: A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of a bulge in his upper abdomen. He first noticed the swelling about a year ago when doing core exercises and says it has become more visible over time, especially when he tenses his abdominal muscles. He reports no pain, nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. His medical history is unremarkable, and he has never had abdominal surgery. He takes no medications and denies weight loss or fevers. On physical examination, a small, firm, non-tender midline mass is palpated between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus. The mass becomes more prominent when the patient is asked to lift his head while lying supine and disappears at rest. There is no erythema, no overlying skin changes, and the rest of the abdominal exam is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with a midline abdominal bulge located between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus, which is characteristic of an epigastric hernia. Epigastric hernias occur when small bowel or preperitoneal fat protrudes through a defect in the linea alba within the epigastrium, typically without a history of trauma or surgery. The defect allows intra-abdominal contents to herniate, forming a visible and sometimes palpable midline mass that becomes more prominent with increased intra-abdominal pressure (e.g., straining, lifting head from supine). The fact that the hernia disappears at rest and is non-tender supports a diagnosis of a reducible hernia rather than a mass lesion or hematoma. Unlike paraumbilical hernias, which occur at or near the umbilical ring, epigastric hernias are higher up in the midline, and their precise location is key to differentiating them. The absence of pain, inflammation, or signs of incarceration suggests an uncomplicated presentation. This patient has no prior surgery or signs of infection, ruling out other structural or acquired lesions. His mass is clearly brought out by tensing the abdominal wall—this maneuver (known as the head-lift or sit-up test) increases intra-abdominal pressure and is helpful in evaluating small hernias. Epigastric hernias can contain fat or bowel but are often small and only clinically significant if symptomatic or enlarging. Elective repair may be considered if symptoms worsen.

subject: MSK
stem: A 70-year-old man comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty using his right hand. He says that over the past several years he has noticed increasing stiffness and trouble extending some of his fingers, which makes it harder to grasp objects and perform daily tasks. He denies any trauma, redness, or acute pain. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic alcohol use. He is retired from construction work and spends most of his time at home. He reports no numbness or tingling but says that his hand sometimes feels tight. He notes the fingers never snap back into place once bent, and the stiffness remains constant rather than intermittent. Examination reveals palpable cords in the palm, and the hand lesion is demonstrated in the image. Sensation and pulses are intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Progressively limited finger extension in an older man with intact sensation and pulses, long-standing diabetes, and chronic alcohol use, together with palpable cords in the palm and the lesion shown in the image, indicates Dupuytren contracture—a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that produces flexion contractures of the ulnar digits. Clinical presentation is insidious, with stiffness, morning tightness, and functional difficulty placing the hand flat on a table (positive tabletop test). The deformity is constant, and unlike trigger finger, there is no intermittent snapping or sudden release. Pain is minimal, neurologic symptoms are absent, and the disability arises from a fixed flexion posture rather than entrapment neuropathy or stenosing tenosynovitis.
