Pathogenesis centers on proliferation of fibroblasts and myofibroblasts within the pretendinous bands of the palmar aponeurosis, driven by profibrotic mediators (eg, TGF-β) and increased type III collagen and fibronectin deposition. Myofibroblast contraction forms nodules and longitudinal cords that progressively shorten the fascia and tether the MCP and PIP joints—most often the ring and little fingers—producing irreversible contracture if untreated. The process is a localized fibroproliferative diathesis rather than an inflammatory arthritis, which explains the paucity of warmth, erythema, or systemic features. Risk is strongly influenced by ancestry and comorbidity. Northern European—classically Norwegian—descent confers a high hereditary predisposition. Alcoholism is the most important modifiable association and correlates with earlier onset and more aggressive disease. Diabetes mellitus increases prevalence via nonenzymatic glycation–linked collagen changes and microvascular factors. Epilepsy is another recognized association, historically linked to anticonvulsant exposure and shared fibrotic pathways. Additional contributors include male sex, age >50, smoking, manual-vibration exposure, and related fibromatoses (eg, plantar fibromatosis, Peyronie disease). Management is staged by functional impairment and joint angles. Mild disease without contracture is observed; hand therapy and nighttime extension splints maintain motion but do not reverse cords. Early, painful nodules may respond to intralesional corticosteroids. For functional contracture, options include collagenase Clostridium histolyticum injection to enzymatically rupture the cord, percutaneous needle aponeurotomy (needle fasciotomy) to divide cords through small punctures, or limited/open fasciectomy for dense, recurrent, or PIP-dominant disease. Intervention is typically recommended for MCP contracture ≥30° or any PIP contracture that interferes with function. Recurrence is common—higher in younger patients, strong diathesis, and PIP involvement—so postoperative therapy, splinting in extension, and longitudinal follow-up are essential.

subject: MSK
stem: A 76-year-old man comes to the clinic due to progressive difficulty hearing over the past year and a sensation of fullness in his right ear. He also reports that he recently had to purchase a larger hat because his old one felt tight. He denies ear pain, discharge, or prior surgeries. His past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and osteoarthritis. He takes metformin and acetaminophen as needed. On examination, there is bilateral frontal bossing and thickening of the skull bones. Neurologic exam reveals decreased hearing on the right but is otherwise normal. Laboratory evaluation shows normal serum calcium, phosphate, and parathyroid hormone levels. Serum alkaline phosphatase is markedly elevated. A lateral skull x- ray reveals thickened calvarium with areas of both lucency and sclerosis, producing a “cotton wool” appearance. The patient is otherwise asymptomatic and denies bone pain or recent fractures. His vitals are stable, and he has no focal tenderness or neurologic deficits. He is referred to endocrinology for further evaluation and counseling. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient’s condition?

options.D: Impaired calcium sensing by the parathyroid glands
options.E: Increased bone turnover due to dysregulated remodeling

answer_text: Increased bone turnover due to dysregulated remodeling
explanation: Paget disease of bone is a chronic skeletal disorder that arises from a defect in the normal process of bone remodeling. In healthy individuals, bone homeostasis is maintained through a coordinated balance between osteoclastic bone resorption and osteoblastic bone formation. In Paget disease, this tightly regulated process becomes disrupted, leading to a sequence of excessive osteoclastic resorption followed by an exaggerated but disorganized osteoblastic response. The initial overactivation of osteoclasts is thought to be triggered by environmental factors (such as viral infections) in genetically predisposed individuals. The osteoblasts attempt to compensate by rapidly depositing new bone, but the result is structurally abnormal woven bone with a mosaic pattern and poor mechanical integrity. The increased bone turnover leads to enlargement and deformity of affected bones, most commonly involving the pelvis, spine, femur, and skull. In the skull, patients may experience enlargement of the calvarium, resulting in hat-size changes and symptoms such as hearing loss due to compression of cranial nerve VIII or involvement of the ossicles. The rapid osteoblastic activity elevates levels of serum alkaline phosphatase, which serves as a useful biochemical marker in Paget disease. However, serum calcium, phosphate, and parathyroid hormone (PTH) levels typically remain normal unless there is concurrent vitamin D deficiency or immobilization-induced hypercalcemia. Radiologic findings, such as thickened cortical bone and mixed lytic and sclerotic lesions, are hallmark features. Histologically, the bone exhibits a chaotic mix of lamellar and woven architecture, and patients are at increased risk for complications such as fractures, bone pain, osteoarthritis, and, rarely, osteosarcoma. The diagnosis is often made incidentally on imaging or due to elevated ALP levels in an asymptomatic patient. Management typically includes bisphosphonates to suppress osteoclast activity and reduce turnover. Understanding the derangement in the remodeling cycle is key to distinguishing Paget disease from other skeletal conditions with elevated ALP, such as hyperparathyroidism, metastatic bone disease, or healing fractures.
bottom_line: Increased bone turnover due to dysregulated remodeling

subject: MSK
stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of progressive right knee pain that began over the past year. The pain worsens after long walks or climbing stairs and improves with rest. He denies morning stiffness lasting longer than 15 minutes and has no joint swelling or redness. He takes no medications and has no significant past medical history. His BMI is 33. Physical exam shows crepitus and tenderness along the medial joint line of the right knee. His fingers show bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. X-rays of the knee reveal asymmetric joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and marginal osteophytes. Synovial fluid analysis reveals a white blood cell count of 900/µL and normal ESR. Which of the following best explains the primary pathological process causing his symptoms?

options.B: Cartilage degeneration with subchondral bone remodeling

answer_text: Cartilage degeneration with subchondral bone remodeling
explanation: Osteoarthritis (OA) results from progressive wear-and-tear of articular cartilage leading to joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation. Loss of cartilage exposes the underlying subchondral bone, triggering remodeling and the appearance of "eburnation"—a polished ivory-like surface. This degeneration is mechanical and age-related, not immune-mediated. The synovial fluid in OA typically shows low WBC count (<2,000/µL), and ESR is usually normal, confirming a non-inflammatory process. Obesity and biomechanical stress accelerate cartilage breakdown and increase OA risk. The asymmetric nature of joint involvement and the presence of Heberden nodes (DIP enlargement) are classic for OA. Inflammatory joint diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA) typically spare the DIPs and present with symmetric involvement. Understanding the difference in underlying mechanisms helps target correct treatments—OA does not benefit from immunosuppressive or steroid therapy. OA progression may be slowed by weight loss and physical therapy. NSAIDs and acetaminophen provide symptom relief but do not halt the structural damage.
bottom_line: Cartilage degeneration with subchondral bone remodeling

subject: MSK
stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after injuring his right knee while playing in a local soccer match. He says he twisted his leg while rapidly pivoting to change direction and immediately felt a sudden popping sensation followed by severe pain and swelling. He was unable to continue playing and had difficulty walking off the field. He denies any recent trauma to the head or any similar prior injuries. On examination, the right knee is swollen with a moderate effusion. There is marked tenderness over the anterior knee. The Lachman test reveals increased anterior displacement of the tibia compared to the contralateral side. The patient has no signs of neurovascular compromise. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This young athlete presents with classic signs of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear, including a non-contact twisting injury, an audible "pop", immediate pain and swelling, and difficulty bearing weight. These are hallmark clinical clues that strongly suggest ACL disruption, especially in high-demand sports such as soccer where rapid pivoting and deceleration are common injury mechanisms. The most telling physical finding in this case is the positive Lachman test, which is the most sensitive and specific physical examination maneuver for diagnosing ACL injury. This test assesses the degree of anterior tibial translation relative to the femur with the knee slightly flexed. Increased motion compared to the unaffected side is diagnostic of ACL laxity. The anterior drawer test is another exam that can be used to assess ACL integrity. ACL injuries result from rotational or hyperextension forces on a planted knee, often without any direct blow. This fits the patient's injury during a pivot maneuver. The presence of effusion soon after injury also supports hemarthrosis, another typical feature of ACL rupture due to injury to the middle geniculate artery. Recognizing ACL tears early allows for timely imaging with MRI and orthopedic referral. Initial management includes rest, ice, compression, and elevation (RICE), with definitive treatment often involving surgical reconstruction for young, active individuals. Physical therapy is also important to restore joint function and prevent long-term instability.

subject: MSK
stem: A 3-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his parents report that he rolled off a couch and fell approximately 40 cm (16 inches) onto a carpeted floor. They state that he cried briefly but then seemed fine, although over the past few hours he has refused to feed and keeps his left arm stiffly at his side. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He is in the 30th percentile for weight, 40th percentile for length, and 50th percentile for head circumference. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and ecchymosis over the left upper arm, with decreased spontaneous movement of that extremity. No other bruising, oral injuries, or retinal findings are noted. A radiograph of the left humerus is shown. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this injury?

options.E: Vitamin D deficiency causing nutritional rickets

explanation: This patient presents with a fracture in a non-ambulatory infant, inconsistent with the reported mechanism of injury. Spiral fractures, particularly of long bones like the humerus, are essentially pathognomonic for child abuse because they require a rotational or torsional force that is unlikely to occur with a simple fall from a low height onto a carpeted surface. In infants younger than six months, accidental injuries are extremely uncommon due to limited mobility, and any fracture—especially with bruising—should raise concern for inflicted injury. The presence of ecchymosis and decreased use of the extremity without a plausible mechanism supports this suspicion. Child abuse should always be considered in infants with injuries that are unusual for their developmental stage, particularly when caregiver explanations are inconsistent with the severity or nature of the findings. Other red flags include multiple fractures at different stages of healing, posterior rib fractures, retinal hemorrhages, and classic metaphyseal lesions. Spiral fractures in young children without clear trauma require evaluation by child protection teams, skeletal survey, and potential social services involvement. Prompt recognition of abuse is critical to prevent further harm and protect the child from ongoing danger.

subject: MSK
stem: A 61-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a 2-month history of difficulty rising from chairs and climbing stairs. He reports no recent trauma but says he has been feeling more fatigued and occasionally short of breath when exerting himself. Medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, both well controlled. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drugs. Physical examination shows symmetric weakness in the deltoid, biceps, and quadriceps muscles bilaterally. There is no rash, sensory loss, or joint swelling. Deep tendon reflexes are preserved. Laboratory results show a creatine kinase level of 3,200 U/L (normal: 22–198), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 72 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis? Electromyography and nerve conduction studies and anti-Jo1 antibodies

options.A: Electromyography and nerve conduction studies and anti-Jo1 antibodies

answer_text: Electromyography and nerve conduction studies and anti-Jo1 antibodies
explanation: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness with markedly elevated creatine kinase and inflammatory markers suggests an inflammatory myopathy such as polymyositis. The next diagnostic step is electromyography/nerve conduction studies to confirm a myopathic pattern, along with myositis-specific serologies such as anti-Jo-1 antibodies. Lumbar puncture, sural nerve biopsy, and brain MRI evaluate neuropathic or central causes and do not fit this proximal myopathic presentation.
bottom_line: Electromyography and nerve conduction studies and anti-Jo1 antibodies

subject: MSK
stem: A 16-year-old boy comes to the clinic due to progressive lower back pain that has worsened over the past 2 months. He says the pain started gradually after he began participating in competitive gymnastics and has been bothering him mostly during extension-based activities such as jumping, backflips, and bridging movements. The pain is localized to the lower lumbar region and does not radiate down the legs. He denies any numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder changes. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the lower lumbar spine. Neurologic examination is normal. While standing upright, the examiner notes a palpable indentation in the midline lower back and a visible prominence just above it. Straight-leg raise is negative. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This adolescent male presents with gradually worsening low back pain exacerbated by hyperextension movements, in the absence of neurologic findings or systemic symptoms. His participation in high-impact sports like gymnastics is a key predisposing factor due to the repeated stress on the lumbar spine. The finding of a step-off on physical exam— described here as a prominence with an indentation below it—suggests vertebral displacement, characteristic of spondylolisthesis. Spondylolisthesis occurs when one vertebral body slips forward relative to the one beneath it, typically due to a defect in the pars interarticularis (commonly from stress fractures or congenital weakness). This forward slippage leads to altered spinal alignment and mechanical back pain. In adolescents, this condition is often due to repetitive hyperextension, especially in sports like gymnastics, weightlifting, or football. The exam finding of a midline step-off is highly suggestive and should prompt imaging. In mild cases without neurological deficits, the presentation may include localized pain and tight hamstrings. As the severity progresses or the degree of slippage increases, radicular symptoms and neurologic compromise can occur. Imaging confirms the diagnosis by showing forward displacement of a vertebral body, most commonly at L5 over S1. Management depends on the degree of slippage and symptom severity. Conservative treatment (rest, activity modification, physical therapy) is often sufficient in mild cases. Surgery is considered in high-grade slips or if there are neurologic deficits.

subject: MSK
stem: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother due to worsening right thigh pain for the past 3 weeks. The pain began insidiously and has become progressively more intense, especially at night. The child has been limping and recently refused to participate in sports activities. He reports occasional low-grade fevers and fatigue. There is no history of trauma or recent travel, but the patient had a mild viral illness 2 months ago. On physical examination, the right thigh is mildly swollen and tender to palpation over the midshaft of the femur, without overlying erythema or warmth. There is no joint effusion or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory results reveal a mild leukocytosis and elevated ESR. An X-ray of the right femur shows a poorly defined diaphyseal lesion with layered periosteal reaction. A biopsy is performed, and the histologic image is shown below. Based on this patient's presentation and histologic findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A painful diaphyseal bone lesion with onion-skin periosteal reaction, systemic symptoms, and biopsy findings consistent with small round blue cells is classic for Ewing sarcoma. It most often affects children and adolescents and can mimic osteomyelitis clinically. The combination of diaphyseal location and layered periosteal reaction is the key discriminator.

subject: MSK
stem: A 32-year-old man presents with vague lateral knee discomfort that worsens with prolonged standing and stair climbing. He reports intermittent instability when walking on uneven ground and difficulty maintaining balance while pivoting. He denies any history of trauma, swelling, or redness. His occupation requires frequent lifting, and he often stands with a wide-based stance. On examination, there is mild bowing of both legs with an increased intercondylar distance. The medial joint line is unremarkable, but tenderness is noted along the lateral aspect of the knee, just below the joint line. No effusion or warmth is appreciated. The varus stress test at 30° flexion elicits discomfort laterally without gapping medially. Gait reveals mild lateral deviation of the mechanical axis during stance phase. Which of the following structures is most likely under chronic strain in this patient?
explanation: The presence of genu varum (bow-legged alignment) in this patient leads to excessive mechanical loading of the medial knee compartment and stretching of the lateral stabilizers. The varus stress test at 30° flexion specifically isolates the fibular collateral ligament (FCL, or lateral collateral ligament), which becomes painful or tender when chronically strained. The lateral tenderness, absence of joint effusion, and preserved stability medially point toward repetitive traction on the fibular collateral ligament as the source of his pain and not meniscal or cruciate involvement. The fibular collateral ligament is a narrow, cord-like structure extending from the lateral epicondyle of the femur to the head of the fibula. Its primary function is to resist varus stress and external tibial rotation, maintaining lateral knee stability. Unlike the medial collateral ligament, the FCL remains extracapsular and does not attach to the meniscus, allowing it to glide freely during knee motion. Chronic varus alignment, such as seen in genu varum, places persistent tension on the ligament, predisposing it to microtears, thickening, and localized tenderness. Genu varum itself can be physiologic in infants and toddlers, but persistent or acquired bowing in adults results from asymmetric joint loading due to osteoarthritis, rickets, Blount disease, or prior trauma. The mechanical axis shifts medially, concentrating compressive forces on the medial tibiofemoral compartment and creating tensile stress on the lateral structures, including the fibular collateral ligament and biceps femoris tendon. Over time, this imbalance contributes to joint degeneration, instability, and lateral soft tissue strain. Diagnosis is clinical, confirmed with stress testing and imaging such as MRI when ligament injury or chronic degeneration is suspected. Management involves correcting mechanical alignment, offloading stress through orthotics or bracing, and physical therapy focused on quadriceps and hamstring balance. In severe deformity or advanced arthritis, high tibial osteotomy or total knee arthroplasty may be indicated. Early intervention prevents progressive joint degeneration and restores functional alignment.

subject: MSK
stem: A 35-year-old man comes to clinic complaining of ongoing weakness and discomfort in his right shoulder. He works as a professional camera operator and often holds heavy equipment close to his chest for long periods. Over the past month, he has noticed increasing difficulty rotating his arm outward and bringing it close to his body when repositioning gear. He denies trauma, dislocation, numbness, or tingling. He says he still manages his job but has become slower and avoids certain angles that aggravate his shoulder. On physical exam, there is no swelling, warmth, or discoloration. Strength and range of motion are preserved in shoulder flexion, extension, and abduction. When tested with the arm at the side, he demonstrates mild weakness during external rotation and reports discomfort when attempting to bring the arm back toward his torso. Neurovascular exam of the upper limb is normal. The empty-can and full-can tests are negative. Plain radiographs of the shoulder are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s findings?

options.A: Axillary nerve compression affecting teres minor
options.C: Infraspinatus injury causing isolated external rotation deficit

answer_text: Axillary nerve compression affecting teres minor
explanation: This 35-year-old man presents with isolated weakness in shoulder external rotation and adduction, without deficits in other planes of motion. His occupation involves prolonged isometric positioning and subtle shoulder movements, particularly with internal adjustments close to the body, which may lead to strain or nerve entrapment. The absence of trauma, numbness, or abduction weakness helps narrow the diagnosis. His physical exam is clean except for mild weakness during external rotation and adduction. The teres minor is a rotator cuff muscle responsible for external (lateral) rotation and adduction of the shoulder. It is innervated by the axillary nerve, which also supplies the deltoid. In contrast to the infraspinatus, which performs external rotation alone, the teres minor contributes to both external rotation and adduction, especially when the arm is at the side. On USMLE, if a patient has difficulty with both of these motions — and other muscles like deltoid and supraspinatus are uninvolved — then the correct localization is to teres minor. The preserved abduction further support this. In this case, the presentation does not include the classic signs of deltoid involvement (such as difficulty abducting past 15– 90°), which would suggest a more proximal or complete axillary nerve lesion. Instead, it is likely a partial axillary nerve involvement or compression at the quadrangular space, selectively affecting the teres minor.
bottom_line: Axillary nerve compression affecting teres minor

subject: MSK
stem: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department complaining of persistent right arm weakness that began two days ago after a minor trauma. He states that he recently returned from a 3-week trip to visit relatives in the Yukon territory of Canada, where he grew up. While there, he and his cousins spent several days hiking rugged trails, hunting, climbing through rocky outcrops, and paddling long distances on canoes and kayaks. He notes that during a climb near the cliffs bordering a river, he slipped and instinctively grabbed a branch to stop his fall, jerking his right arm forcefully. He felt a sudden pull and pain in the front of the arm, which he initially dismissed as a muscle strain. Later, while helping pack and lift furniture in preparation for the sale of his childhood home, he noticed increasing pain and weakness when bending his elbow or lifting with that arm. His symptoms worsened during the drive back into town, especially after prolonged gripping of the steering wheel. He denies any neck pain, paresthesia in the other arm, or systemic symptoms. He has no history of neurologic illness, trauma, or surgery. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and reports that he is normally very physically active. He has been back in town for 3 days and is concerned that the weakness is not improving. He says he cannot “curl” his arm at the gym, and when trying to bring food to his mouth with that arm, it feels heavy and uncoordinated. He also notes that turning a doorknob or twisting a screwdriver is difficult with that arm, though his hand seems to work fine otherwise. He has no problems with writing, buttoning a shirt, or holding tools, but says his right forearm feels “numb” when touched on the outer side. He does not recall dropping objects but says he hasn’t been using that arm much since the symptoms began. On exam, the right arm shows no visible deformities, abrasions, or ecchymoses. There is localized tenderness and mild swelling in the anterior upper arm. Motor testing shows marked weakness in elbow flexion and forearm supination. Wrist extension, finger flexion, and grip strength are intact. Shoulder abduction and internal/external rotation are preserved. Sensory exam reveals decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the forearm, with intact sensation elsewhere. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric except for a slightly diminished biceps reflex on the right. X-ray of the arm shows no fractures or dislocations. Based on his exam, imaging, and mechanism of injury, the patient is diagnosed with a peripheral nerve injury. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?
explanation: The musculocutaneous nerve, originating from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus (C5–C7), innervates the anterior compartment of the arm—specifically the biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis muscles—which are essential for elbow flexion and forearm supination. It also provides sensory innervation to the lateral forearm via the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm. This patient’s injury likely occurred during the forceful arm traction when he grabbed a branch to break his fall while climbing, a classic mechanism for stretch or traction injury to the musculocutaneous nerve. His symptoms of impaired elbow flexion and supination, combined with lateral forearm sensory loss and an otherwise intact shoulder, hand, and wrist, are classic for this isolated nerve injury. Because the musculocutaneous nerve branches off high in the brachial plexus and travels deep within the upper arm, isolated injury is rare but can occur with trauma or excessive strain. In this case, his active lifestyle—including lifting, climbing, and paddling—exposed him to high risk of a stretch injury or compression neuropathy, especially with prolonged arm flexion and gripping. The biceps and brachialis are key to elbow flexion, and the biceps additionally helps with forearm supination. His difficulty with activities like eating, turning doorknobs, or curling weights further supports biceps weakness. The slightly diminished biceps reflex further localizes the issue. The absence of deficits in grip strength, finger extension, wrist flexion, or shoulder abduction, as well as the normal findings on x-ray, help rule out injury to the radial, ulnar, or axillary nerves, as well as cervical root involvement. Most musculocutaneous nerve injuries are neurapraxias and resolve spontaneously over time with conservative management, including physical therapy and activity modification. However, prolonged symptoms may require electrodiagnostic studies to evaluate for axonal damage. The early recognition of this injury is important in active patients like this one to ensure appropriate rehabilitation and full functional recovery.

subject: MSK
stem: A 44-year-old woman is referred to neurology for evaluation of persistent left posterior thigh weakness. Six months ago, she underwent surgery for a large posterior thigh liposarcoma that required careful dissection near the sciatic nerve. Following the procedure, she noticed difficulty extending her hip and flexing her knee. There was also mild atrophy of the left hamstring region. Over the following months, she participated in physical therapy and regained partial strength, but repeat electromyography was ordered to assess reinnervation progress. On neurologic examination, there is residual weakness in the left biceps femoris and semimembranosus muscles with noticeable thinning of the posterior thigh. Deep tendon reflexes are intact. Nerve conduction studies show evidence of reinnervation, and needle EMG of the left hamstring muscles reveals motor units that appear histologically homogeneous—containing clusters of similarly staining muscle fibers. These findings are consistent with chronic neurogenic remodeling following partial reinnervation. Which of the following histologic findings is most likely present in this patient’s recovering muscle?

options.C: Increased mitochondrial density in type I fibers
options.D: Peripheral displacement of nuclei with ringed fibers
options.E: Type I and II cells fiber type grouping

answer_text: Type I and II cells fiber type grouping
explanation: This patient presents with weakness following surgical manipulation near the sciatic nerve, which innervates the hamstring muscles. The described symptoms—difficulty flexing the knee and extending the hip—are consistent with denervation of muscles such as the biceps femoris and semimembranosus. Over time, improvement in strength and EMG findings suggest partial reinnervation of these muscles, which helps us narrow down the underlying pathophysiology. During reinnervation, surviving motor neurons sprout collateral branches to reinnervate nearby denervated fibers. Importantly, the motor neuron determines the muscle fiber’s type (I or II). This leads to fiber type grouping, in which muscle fibers of the same type become clustered together instead of being distributed in a checkerboard pattern. This reorganization is pathognomonic of neurogenic recovery rather than a primary myopathy. The key histologic finding in this scenario is clusters of histochemically similar muscle fibers. This occurs because regenerating axons reinnervate nearby denervated fibers, creating units that all adopt the metabolic and contractile identity of the reinnervating neuron. It is not an adaptive change in metabolism or exercise physiology but rather a direct consequence of motor unit plasticity. In this case, the presence of similar staining motor units on EMG, along with the clinical history of a nerve injury and signs of gradual motor recovery, supports the diagnosis of fiber type grouping.
bottom_line: Type I and II cells fiber type grouping

subject: MSK
stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive muscle fatigue and weakness over the past few months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a seated position, and lifting objects above his head. His symptoms are symmetrical and more pronounced in the proximal muscles. He has no sensory deficits, no fasciculations, and deep tendon reflexes are preserved. Laboratory studies reveal mildly elevated creatine kinase levels, and electromyography demonstrates a myopathic pattern. Muscle biopsy shows fiber size variation and increased central nuclei. Electron microscopy reveals that certain regions of the sarcomere shorten during contraction, while others, specifically one band, maintain a constant length throughout. Which of the following labeled regions on the image remains unchanged in length during muscle contraction?
explanation: The A band remains constant in length during muscle contraction because it corresponds to the full length of the thick myosin filaments. In contrast, the I band and H zone shorten as thin actin filaments slide inward. On the labeled image, region 2 corresponds to the A band.

subject: MSK
stem: A 3-week-old newborn boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a firm mass on the right side of his neck that they first noticed a few days ago. The baby was born at 39 weeks via forceps-assisted vaginal delivery after a prolonged second stage of labor. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, and the baby passed the newborn screening tests. The parents deny any recent fever, fussiness, feeding difficulties, or signs of infection. They state that the baby tends to keep his head turned slightly to the left, and they have noticed some asymmetry in how he holds his head when awake. On physical examination, a firm, non-tender 2-cm mass is palpable in the right lateral neck. The mass does not move with swallowing or tongue protrusion and there is no overlying skin change or drainage. Neurologic examination is normal, and the baby feeds and interacts appropriately for his age. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Fibromatosis colli from birth-related injury to the sternocleidomastoid muscle

options.A: Branchial cleft cyst resulting from failed obliteration of embryonic cleft
options.B: Congenital dermoid cyst arising from ectodermal entrapment
options.C: Fibromatosis colli from birth-related injury to the sternocleidomastoid muscle
options.D: Midline mass due to persistent thyroglossal duct remnant
options.E: Reactive cervical lymphadenopathy secondary to recent viral infection

answer_text: Fibromatosis colli from birth-related injury to the sternocleidomastoid muscle
explanation: This infant presents with a firm, lateral neck mass associated with a history of forceps-assisted delivery and postural preference for head turning, which is classic for fibromatosis colli, a benign condition resulting from traumatic injury to the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle during birth. Forceps or vacuum extraction, especially after a prolonged or difficult labor, can result in localized hemorrhage and fibrosis within the SCM. The firm mass reflects this fibrosis and typically appears within the first few weeks of life. Importantly, the mass is located laterally, is non-tender, and does not move with swallowing or tongue protrusion, helping differentiate it from thyroglossal duct cysts and other midline lesions. The associated torticollis—head tilt toward the affected side and chin rotation to the opposite side—is another supporting feature. There are no signs of infection or systemic illness, reinforcing a traumatic origin. Diagnosis is often clinical, although ultrasound can be used to confirm the lesion and assess for muscle fibrosis. The condition is self-limited, and the mainstay of treatment is conservative management with physical therapy, which helps restore range of motion and prevent facial asymmetry. Most cases resolve completely within a few months.
bottom_line: Fibromatosis colli from birth-related injury to the sternocleidomastoid muscle

subject: MSK
stem: A 16-year-old boy presents to the clinic after falling while skateboarding. He describes landing on his outstretched right hand and feeling immediate discomfort. Since the injury, he has had persistent pain in the central palm, with no numbness, tingling, or motor weakness. He denies any pain in the thumb or near the base of the thumb. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the central volar wrist, and no tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Range of motion of the wrist and thumb is mildly limited due to discomfort, but neurovascular examination is normal. The labeled radiograph is shown below. Which of the labeled structures (A–H) is most likely fractured in this patient?
explanation: This adolescent presents with wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH), a common mechanism for carpal bone fractures. The key distinguishing feature in this case is pain localized to the central palm with no tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox, which effectively rules out scaphoid fracture. In contrast, central volar wrist pain is highly suggestive of injury to the lunate, the centrally positioned bone in the proximal carpal row. The lunate is vulnerable to axial compressive forces, and lunate fractures or dislocations can result from FOOSH injuries, especially when the wrist is in extension. These injuries may be subtle on X-ray and easily missed unless clinical suspicion is high. Lunate injury can also lead to avascular necrosis, making early identification crucial. On the labeled X-ray, label D corresponds to the lunate, located just distal to the radius and medial to the scaphoid. USMLE often tests wrist anatomy by presenting subtle distinctions like central vs. radial pain, expecting students to know carpal bone positions and injury patterns. In this case, the absence of snuffbox tenderness and the presence of central palm pain directly indicate lunate pathology, making D the correct choice.

subject: MSK
stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic due to several months of neck pain that has gradually worsened. He describes the discomfort as a dull, aching sensation in the posterior neck that sometimes radiates to the shoulders, particularly after long periods of desk work or driving. He denies any trauma, recent infections, or constitutional symptoms. He also reports occasional stiffness in the morning lasting about 10–15 minutes but says it improves throughout the day. There is no history of numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. He has well-controlled hypertension and takes lisinopril. Neurological examination is normal, with preserved strength and sensation in all extremities. Reflexes are symmetric. MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes involving the vertebral bodies and intervertebral discs, with osteophyte formation and mild narrowing of the neural foramina at multiple levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Cervical spondylosis refers to age-related degeneration of the cervical spine and is a common cause of chronic neck pain in older adults, particularly those over age 50. The condition involves disc degeneration, osteophyte formation, and sometimes narrowing of the neural foramina or spinal canal. This patient’s presentation of progressive, non-radiating neck pain with stiffness after inactivity and MRI showing degenerative vertebral changes strongly aligns with cervical spondylosis. Importantly, cervical spondylosis can present with or without neurologic findings. In this case, the absence of radicular symptoms or neurologic deficits suggests a milder form limited to mechanical degeneration. The finding of neural foraminal narrowing without spinal cord compression supports this. The chronicity and pattern of stiffness (brief, improving with movement) also favor a degenerative mechanical origin rather than an inflammatory condition. While spondylosis can occur in the lumbar spine, the cervical region is more commonly tested on USMLE. The condition involves degeneration of the vertebral body, including the pars interarticularis.

subject: MSK
stem: A 38-year-old man comes to the outpatient clinic with a 6-week history of right elbow discomfort that has gradually worsened. He works as a delivery driver. He says the pain is localized to the outer part of his elbow and is most noticeable when lifting boxes or turning doorknobs. The discomfort is particularly pronounced when he tries to extend his wrist while holding objects, such as a coffee mug. He denies numbness, tingling, or radiation of the pain down his arm. He has no prior joint disorders and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lateral aspect of the right elbow. The pain is reproduced when the patient extends his wrist against resistance with the elbow fully extended. There is no swelling or erythema over the joint, and range of motion is preserved. The neurovascular exam of the right upper extremity is normal. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology in this patient’s condition? Compression of the posterior interosseous nerve within the supinator canal

options.A: Injury to the medial epicondylar origin of the pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis
options.B: Inflammation at the origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis
options.C: Inflammation of the bursa overlying the olecranon process
options.D: Compression of the posterior interosseous nerve within the supinator canal
options.E: Ulnar nerve entrapment in the cubital tunnel

answer_text: Inflammation at the origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis
explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of lateral epicondylitis, also known as tennis elbow. He reports localized pain over the lateral elbow that is aggravated by activities involving wrist extension against resistance, such as lifting objects or turning doorknobs. The absence of neurologic symptoms or swelling helps rule out neuropathy or joint effusion. Lateral epicondylitis is caused by repetitive strain and microtrauma at the origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) tendon, where it inserts at the lateral epicondyle of the humerus. The ECRB is particularly vulnerable because it functions to stabilize the wrist during extension, especially during gripping or lifting motions with the elbow extended. Inflammation and degeneration at this tendon insertion result in pain and tenderness over the lateral elbow. The physical exam finding of pain with resisted wrist extension is highly specific for lateral epicondylitis. Additionally, preserved range of motion and absence of swelling or erythema make infectious or systemic causes less likely. The fact that the patient performs repetitive wrist movements for work and in tennis increases the likelihood of ECRB overuse injury. Management typically involves activity modification, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and physical therapy. A counterforce forearm strap may be used to reduce stress on the tendon origin.
bottom_line: Inflammation at the origin of the extensor carpi radialis brevis

subject: MSK
stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute left knee pain and swelling that began overnight. He reports two prior episodes affecting the same knee over the last three years, both of which resolved without intervention. His medical history is notable for hypertension and daily alcohol consumption. On examination, the left knee is swollen, erythematous, warm, and tender with limited range of motion. Vital signs are within normal limits except for a temperature of 37.5°C (99.5°F). Synovial fluid is obtained for analysis. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient’s condition? Autoimmune-mediated synovial hyperplasia with pannus formation

options.A: Autoimmune-mediated synovial hyperplasia with pannus formation
options.B: Cartilage erosion due to repetitive mechanical stress
options.C: Deposition of circulating immune complexes in the synovium
options.E: Synovial invasion by pathogens triggering neutrophilic inflammation

explanation: Gout is caused by supersaturation of uric acid in the serum, which leads to precipitation of monosodium urate crystals within synovial fluid. The classic site of involvement is the first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint, but other large joints such as the knee, ankle, and midfoot can also be affected. The disease occurs in flares marked by sudden-onset monoarthritis characterized by severe pain, erythema, swelling, and limited range of motion. Risk factors include chronic alcohol use, red meat or seafood-heavy diets, obesity, thiazide or loop diuretic use, and renal insufficiency. Importantly, hyperuricemia is neither necessary nor sufficient for the diagnosis; patients with normal serum urate can have gout(i.e uric acid levels fluctuate so they can normalize by the time the blood sample is taken), and many with elevated levels never develop it. Pathophysiologically, urate crystals activate the NLRP3 inflammasome within macrophages, leading to cleavage of pro–IL-1β and release of active IL-1β, which drives a rapid neutrophilic inflammatory cascade. Diagnosis is confirmed by joint aspiration showing needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light. Acute flares are treated with NSAIDs first-line, followed by corticosteroids or colchicine depending on contraindications. Chronic management focuses on lowering serum uric acid levels using xanthine oxidase inhibitors such as allopurinol or febuxostat, which must be avoided during acute attacks to prevent paradoxical worsening. Failure to treat recurrent flares can lead to chronic tophaceous gout and joint destruction.

subject: MSK
stem: A 64-year-old woman presents to the clinic with gradually worsening knee pain over the past 6 months. The pain is worse with activity and improves with rest. She finds it difficult to get up after prolonged sitting and complains that her knees feel stiff in the morning but improve within 20 minutes. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She has smoked one pack per day for the last 35 years and has a BMI of 33 kg/m². On examination, there is mild crepitus and tenderness along the medial joint line of both knees but no erythema or effusion. Her gait is normal, and her neurological exam is unremarkable. Radiographs show joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in the knees. The patient is interested in reducing the long-term burden of her symptoms. Which of the following is the most effective intervention to reduce her risk of disease progression?
explanation: Obesity is a major modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of osteoarthritis (OA), particularly in weight-bearing joints such as the knees. Losing as little as 5–10% of body weight has been shown to significantly reduce joint loading and inflammation, thereby decreasing symptoms and halting further cartilage degradation. On USMLE, weight loss is the best long-term preventive strategy in overweight patients with OA. The clinical presentation of OA includes activity-related joint pain that is relieved by rest, morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes, and reduced range of motion. Examination may show joint line tenderness, crepitus, and sometimes bony enlargements like Heberden (Bony enlargements of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints) or Bouchard nodes (Bony enlargements of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints). Management of OA starts with nonpharmacologic interventions such as weight loss and exercise. For pharmacologic treatment, acetaminophen is a first-line treatment (OA is non inflammatory disease) on USMLE, especially in patients who cannot tolerate NSAIDs. NSAIDs (oral or topical) are also widely used for pain control, but should be approached with caution in patients with cardiovascular or gastrointestinal comorbidities. In more severe cases, intraarticular glucocorticoid injections or even arthroplasty may be necessary.

subject: MSK
stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He was the front-seat passenger and was not wearing a seatbelt. According to EMS, the car struck a guardrail and spun into a ditch. The patient was alert at the scene but complained of severe pain in the right hip and was unable to bear weight. He denies head trauma, loss of consciousness, or abdominal pain. There is no bleeding, open wound, or deformity of the thigh. On physical examination, the right leg appears shortened and held in a position of internal rotation at the hip. Passive range of motion is limited by pain. There is no sensory loss or distal neurovascular compromise. Radiographs are pending, but the orthopedic resident is consulted for evaluation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: A high-speed motor vehicle collision with a shortened, internally rotated leg is the classic presentation of posterior hip dislocation. Posterior dislocations are more common than anterior dislocations and often occur when force drives the femur posteriorly while the hip is flexed. Rapid reduction is required to reduce the risk of avascular necrosis.

subject: MSK
stem: A 26-year-old man presents to the emergency department after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was the restrained front-seat passenger in a car that struck a wall head-on. He recalls that his right knee hit the dashboard upon impact. He denies loss of consciousness but reports immediate pain and swelling in the right knee. On examination, there is a moderate joint effusion. The knee is not obviously deformed, and there is no visible bruising. When the patient lies supine with both hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, the right tibia appears to sit farther back compared to the left. Gentle manipulation of the knee confirms increased backward movement of the tibia relative to the femur. The patient is otherwise hemodynamically stable and has no other injuries. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient sustained a dashboard injury in a head-on collision in which his proximal tibia struck the dashboard while the knee was flexed, producing a force directed posteriorly along the tibia. This is the classic mechanism of a posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) injury, which stabilizes the tibia and prevents it from being displaced backward relative to the femur. The PCL is the strongest ligament in the knee, and it typically requires high-energy trauma to rupture. In this case, the patient has a posteriorly displaced tibia on inspection with the knees flexed, and there is increased posterior translation of the tibia with gentle pressure — consistent with a positive posterior drawer test. These findings are pathognomonic for a PCL tear. Swelling and joint effusion are common due to hemarthrosis or associated capsular injury. Unlike ACL tears, which are often non-contact and present with instability while pivoting or changing direction, PCL injuries result from a direct blow to the tibia. In motor vehicle accidents, this frequently occurs when the shin strikes the dashboard. Physical exam findings center around posterior laxity, not anterior movement or rotational instability. MRI is used to confirm the diagnosis and assess for associated soft tissue damage.

subject: MSK
stem: A 25-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to pain in his right hand after falling off his bicycle earlier today. He was riding downhill at moderate speed when he lost control and landed on the handlebars with his body weight transmitted through the palm of his right hand. He did not hit his head or lose consciousness. He has no other complaints. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the hypothenar eminence. Grip strength is slightly reduced due to pain, and tapping over the affected region elicits tingling radiating to the ring and little fingers. There is no visible swelling, ecchymosis, or open wound. The patient is right-hand dominant and otherwise healthy. His wrist range of motion is intact, and capillary refill and radial pulses are normal. X-ray of the wrist is initially read as normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with hand pain localized to the hypothenar eminence after falling on the handlebar, with symptoms of ulnar nerve irritation (e.g., paresthesia of the fourth and fifth digits), which is classic for a hook of hamate fracture. These fractures typically occur when a fall or blunt force is transmitted through the palm, such as from gripping handlebars or sports equipment (e.g., baseball bat, tennis racket, or golf club). The hook of hamate lies on the ulnar side of the wrist and forms part of the Guyon canal, through which the ulnar nerve travels. Injury to this area can thus result in both pain over the hypothenar area and ulnar nerve symptoms, such as tingling in the ring and little fingers and weakness in grip. These fractures are often radiographically occult on plain films, and advanced imaging (CT/MRI) may be needed. In addition, neurologic symptoms in the ulnar distribution after handlebar or racquet sports injuries should immediately raise suspicion for hook of hamate fracture. Initial management includes immobilization in a cast; surgical excision may be required if symptoms persist.

subject: MSK
stem: A 49-year-old man presents to the clinic with anterior knee pain and swelling that has developed gradually over the past week. He works as a plumber and spends much of his workday kneeling while installing fixtures. He says the knee feels sore and tight but not unstable, and he denies any trauma, locking, or popping. The pain is mostly noticeable when he kneels or presses directly on the front of his knee, and it does not limit his ability to walk or climb stairs. He denies fever, chills, or recent illness. He has no history of joint disease or gout. On physical examination, there is a soft, mildly tender swelling located over the front of the right knee, just above the patellar tendon. The overlying skin is intact with no erythema or warmth. Range of motion is full and non-painful. There is no joint effusion or ligamentous laxity. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The diagnosis in this case is prepatellar bursitis, a condition characterized by inflammation of the bursa located between the patella and the overlying skin. This commonly occurs in individuals who kneel frequently, such as plumbers, tile workers, or gardeners, due to repetitive friction and pressure on the anterior knee. The patient in this vignette reports gradual-onset anterior knee pain with focal swelling, and he works in a kneeling-intensive occupation, making this diagnosis highly likely. On physical examination, the patient has localized swelling over the front of the knee that is soft and tender, but without signs of infection or joint involvement. The swelling is anterior and superficial, and his range of motion is intact, which helps distinguish bursitis from intra-articular pathology. The absence of systemic symptoms such as fever, and lack of erythema or warmth, makes septic bursitis or cellulitis unlikely. His description of pain specifically during kneeling is consistent with inflammation of the prepatellar bursa. Prepatellar bursitis (also known as housemaid’s knee) is often caused by chronic repetitive trauma to the anterior knee. This leads to inflammation and fluid accumulation in the bursa without direct joint involvement. Unlike anserine bursitis, which presents medially and is often related to obesity or OA, prepatellar bursitis affects the anterior surface of the knee and is typically work-related. Diagnosis is clinical and can be confirmed by history and exam alone. Initial management involves NSAIDs and activity modification, with ice or compression used as needed. Aspiration is reserved for suspected infection or persistent cases. Most cases resolve without invasive intervention.

subject: MSK
stem: A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain in her right wrist for the past 6 days. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the outer (thumb- side) part of the wrist, and says it has made it increasingly difficult to carry out certain tasks like picking up her newborn daughter, who is 7 weeks old. The discomfort started mildly but has now progressed to the point where even turning a doorknob or lifting a coffee mug causes discomfort. She denies numbness, tingling, trauma, fever, or joint swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable. She is exclusively breastfeeding and has recently increased the frequency and duration of feedings. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lateral (radial) wrist, just proximal to the base of the thumb. There is no visible swelling or redness, and full passive range of motion is preserved. However, the patient experiences a sudden sharp pain when asked to tuck her thumb into her palm, wrap her fingers around it, and bend her wrist toward the ulna. No other joint involvement is noted, and neurologic examination of the upper limb is normal. Which of the following best describes this patient’s condition? A. Entrapment of a peripheral nerve at the wrist B. Inflammation of tendon sheaths controlling thumb movement C. Joint capsule inflammation involving the radiocarpal articulation D. Postpartum overuse injury of a flexor muscle insertion E. Systemic inflammatory condition with asymmetric joint involvement

options.A: Median nerve compression within the carpal tunnel
options.C: Inflammation of the wrist joint capsule
options.D: Overuse injury of the wrist flexor tendons

explanation: This patient presents with localized lateral wrist pain in the context of repetitive hand use while breastfeeding, most notably worsened by the Finkelstein maneuver, which strongly suggests de Quervain tenosynovitis. The condition is characterized by inflammation of the tendon sheaths of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis muscles, which pass through the first dorsal compartment of the wrist. These tendons facilitate thumb abduction and extension, and inflammation leads to pain with repetitive thumb movements, especially in postpartum women due to frequent lifting and wrist deviation. The Finkelstein test is highly specific and was clearly positive in this case. It involves placing the thumb inside a closed fist and ulnar deviating the wrist. The radial-sided pain reproduced by this maneuver, combined with the patient’s risk factor (breastfeeding with repetitive wrist motion), confirms the diagnosis.

subject: MSK
stem: A 33-year-old violinist comes to the clinic with progressive pain and stiffness in her fingers, especially in the morning. Over the past 4 months, she has found it increasingly difficult to play her instrument due to finger joint discomfort. She reports swelling and warmth over several knuckles but denies any recent trauma or fever. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling, tenderness, and erythema of the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints. The distal interphalangeal joints are not affected. Hand radiographs show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. If a synovial biopsy were obtained, which of the following histopathologic findings would most likely be seen?

options.C: Focal loss of hyaline cartilage with subchondral bone thickening

explanation: This patient has classic findings of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), characterized by prolonged morning stiffness and symmetric involvement of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints, with sparing of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints. RA is a clinical diagnosis supported by serologies (positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies) and imaging findings such as joint space narrowing and marginal erosions, as seen in this patient. Synovial fluid analysis typically shows sterile inflammation (WBC 2,000–50,000/mm³, no crystals). Although synovial biopsy is not routinely performed, the hallmark histologic feature is synovial pannus, which consists of granulation tissue composed of fibroblasts, inflammatory cells, and neovascularization. This invasive tissue erodes cartilage and bone, driving the characteristic joint destruction in RA.

subject: MSK
stem: A 62-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent left hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past 5 months. He says the discomfort is dull and deep, often worse at night, and no longer improves with over-the-counter medications. He denies trauma, fever, or recent infection. Imaging demonstrates a destructive cartilaginous lesion of the pelvis/hip region. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with slowly progressive deep bone pain, worse at night, and imaging findings consistent with a lytic, calcified lesion involving the pelvis. The histopathology reveals neoplastic chondrocytes embedded in lacunae, a classic appearance for chondrosarcoma — a malignant cartilage-forming tumor seen in older adults. The histo image is extremely buzzy for USMLE. It often arises in flat bones like the pelvis, femur, or proximal humerus and can appear radiographically as lytic lesions with “rings and arcs” or stippled calcifications, which represent mineralized cartilage. Chondrosarcomas grow slowly but are locally aggressive and may cause cortical destruction or soft tissue extension. Grossly, they are described as having a glistening, bluish-gray appearance, due to the cartilage content. Histologically, they demonstrate malignant cartilage cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, variable mitoses, and cells embedded within lacunae in a cartilaginous matrix, exactly as seen in the image provided. Patients typically report persistent pain that worsens over time and may be severe at night. Unlike osteoid osteoma, the pain is not typically responsive to NSAIDs. MRI and CT help define the lesion, but diagnosis is confirmed via biopsy. Management often requires surgical resection, as these tumors tend to resist chemotherapy and radiation.

subject: MSK
stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a 3-month history of a painful, swollen skull lesion. Imaging and pathology are consistent with Langerhans cell histiocytosis. This patient’s condition is most likely caused by malignant proliferation of which of the following cell types?
explanation: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare clonal proliferative disorder characterized by the accumulation of pathologic Langerhans cells, which are specialized dendritic cells normally involved in antigen presentation in the skin and mucosa. This condition typically affects children and can present with lytic bone lesions, such as in the skull, mandible, or long bones, leading to localized pain and swelling. Other manifestations can include skin rashes, otitis media, and, in disseminated cases, systemic symptoms like fever and organ dysfunction. Histologically, these abnormal cells are positive for CD1a positive and display distinctive intracytoplasmic inclusions called Birbeck granules. Under electron microscopy, Birbeck granules appear as rod-shaped or tennis racquet–shaped structures unique to Langerhans cells and help confirm the diagnosis of LCH. These granules are believed to be involved in receptor-mediated endocytosis.

subject: MSK
stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to progressive limping and intermittent right hip pain for the past 3 weeks. His mother reports that he has been otherwise well, with no recent fever, trauma, or illness. The pain initially occurred only after prolonged running or playing but has now become more persistent. He has no complaints of pain in his knee or foot. The patient denies night sweats, weight loss, or malaise. He has no history of travel, tick bites, or recent infections. His past medical history is unremarkable, and his immunizations are up to date. He is not taking any medications. His academic and social development are age appropriate. There is no family history of rheumatologic or orthopedic conditions. On examination, he is afebrile and appears well. He walks with an antalgic gait, favoring the right side. There is no visible swelling, redness, or warmth of the hip. Passive internal rotation and abduction of the right hip are limited and mildly painful. The left hip and both knees are normal on examination. Neurovascular status is intact bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal normal ESR, CRP, and white blood cell count. Plain radiographs of the pelvis show increased density and slight flattening of the right femoral head, while the left femoral head appears normal. There is no joint space widening or signs of effusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is a condition of idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head, most commonly seen in boys between ages 4 and 10 (but especially 5-8 range, as per my observation across NBMEs). It arises due to interruption of blood supply to the capital femoral epiphysis, which leads to necrosis, collapse, and gradual remodeling of the femoral head over time. The condition presents subacutely, often with a limp and mild hip or groin pain that progresses over weeks. On physical examination, children typically have limited internal rotation and abduction of the affected hip, and may hold the hip in slight flexion or abduction. Systemic symptoms such as fever or malaise are usually absent, and laboratory studies—including ESR, CRP, and WBC count—are normal, helping distinguish it from infectious or inflammatory causes. Radiographic changes in Legg-Calvé-Perthes evolve through several stages: initial sclerosis due to infarction, fragmentation, reossification, and remodeling. In early disease, X-rays may show increased radiodensity of the femoral head compared to the contralateral side, along with subtle flattening or collapse. MRI is more sensitive in detecting early ischemic changes and may be used if the radiograph is inconclusive but clinical suspicion remains high. The disease is typically unilateral and affects otherwise healthy children with no preceding trauma or infection. Pain may be referred to the ipsilateral thigh or knee, which can sometimes delay diagnosis. Management depends on the age at presentation and the extent of femoral head involvement. In children under 6 with mild disease, observation and activity restriction are often sufficient, as spontaneous revascularization and remodeling occur. In older children or those with extensive collapse, treatment may involve bracing, casting, or surgery to preserve femoral head shape and maintain joint congruence. Physical therapy is critical to preserve range of motion. The prognosis is generally favorable in younger children but can lead to early-onset osteoarthritis if the femoral head does not heal properly. Long-term follow-up is necessary to monitor hip development and function.

subject: MSK
stem: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of gradually worsening stiffness in her fingers, difficulty opening jars, and a persistent dull ache in both wrists. She says the symptoms are worst in the morning and often make it hard to get dressed, though they improve somewhat over the course of the day. She has also noticed that her wedding ring feels tighter than usual, and she occasionally feels tingling in her palms. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness at the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally, with restricted wrist flexion due to discomfort. Tinel and Phalen maneuvers reproduce her paresthesias. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following structures is most likely contributing to the nerve-related symptoms in this patient?
explanation: This patient likely has rheumatoid arthritis complicated by median nerve compression in the carpal tunnel, as suggested by inflammatory MCP/wrist synovitis plus positive Tinel and Phalen maneuvers. The transverse carpal ligament forms the roof of the carpal tunnel and contributes to compression of the median nerve when synovial swelling narrows the tunnel. That structure is therefore the key contributor to her paresthesias.

subject: MSK
stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 16-hour history of severe pain in his left first metatarsophalangeal joint. He has a past medical history of hypertension and obesity. He reports drinking several beers at a party the night before the pain started. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and exquisitely tender big toe joint with markedly restricted range of motion. He is afebrile and denies any recent trauma or history of autoimmune disease. Laboratory studies are negative for rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies. A joint aspirate is shown. What is the most immediate physiological mechanism responsible for the development of this patient's joint swelling?
explanation: Increased vascular permeability is the key physiologic mechanism responsible for the rapid accumulation of neutrophils and subsequent swelling in acute gouty arthritis. Monosodium urate crystals deposited in the synovial fluid are recognized by the innate immune system, primarily through activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome in macrophages, which leads to the release of IL-1β. This cytokine, along with other pro-inflammatory mediators (e.g., TNF-α, IL-8), induces local endothelial cells to increase vascular permeability, allowing plasma proteins and neutrophils to exit the circulation and enter the synovial space. The resultant influx of neutrophils amplifies the inflammatory response and produces the hallmark erythema, swelling, and pain of acute gout. The needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals seen under polarized light microscopy are characteristic of monosodium urate, not calcium pyrophosphate (which would be rhomboid and positively birefringent in pseudogout). The acute inflammatory response is dominated by the innate immune system and neutrophils rather than adaptive immunity; the primary event is extravasation of fluid and leukocytes due to increased vascular permeability, not T-cell activation or immune complex formation. This mechanism is a classic example of an exudative process central to many types of acute inflammation. Acute gout is managed with NSAIDs (e.g., indomethacin), colchicine (within 36 hours), or corticosteroids if NSAIDs are contraindicated. Rest, ice, and hydration are supportive. Uric acid-lowering therapy (e.g., allopurinol) is avoided during acute attacks to prevent symptom worsening.

subject: MSK
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a six-month history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness. She describes the discomfort as a dull, aching sensation that began in her fingers and wrists and has now started affecting her ankles as well. The stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and often lasts more than an hour before it gradually improves with activity. She says that on some mornings, she struggles to brush her teeth or hold a coffee mug due to pain and reduced grip strength. By the end of the day, the discomfort is milder, but it flares up again the following morning. She denies any recent trauma or overuse. In addition to the joint symptoms, she reports generalized fatigue and difficulty concentrating at work. She has also unintentionally lost around 3 kilograms over the past few months, despite no changes in her diet or exercise routine. She has no personal history of psoriasis, gastrointestinal issues, or genitourinary infections. She does not recall having a recent sore throat or viral illness. There is no family history of autoimmune disease, though her mother has osteoarthritis. She does not smoke or drink and works as a graphic designer. On further questioning, she denies any fever, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers. She has not had any miscarriages or issues with fertility. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. Her vital signs are normal. There is mild symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints and the proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her wrists are slightly puffy and tender to palpation with limited range of motion. There is no redness or warmth over the joints. Her distal interphalangeal joints are unaffected. Her ankles are also mildly swollen, with some tenderness on passive dorsiflexion, but no signs of enthesitis. There is no visible skin rash, no nail pitting, and no scalp lesions. The rest of her examination is unremarkable. Laboratory testing reveals anemia and an elevated inflammatory marker profile. Radiographs show soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia without erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease that primarily affects the synovial joints in a symmetric and progressive fashion. It most commonly presents in women aged 20–40 and is characterized by morning stiffness lasting more than one hour, symmetric small-joint involvement (especially the MCP, PIP, and wrists), and systemic symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, and anemia. The disease results from chronic synovial inflammation leading to joint damage over time. Diagnostic testing often reveals elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), positive rheumatoid factor (RF), and highly specific anti–cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Radiographic findings in early disease may include soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia; later findings include marginal erosions and joint space narrowing. RA must be distinguished from other causes of inflammatory arthritis by its chronicity, joint distribution, and serologic markers. For example, osteoarthritis spares the MCP joints and has brief morning stiffness, while psoriatic and reactive arthritis often involve DIP joints or entheses and present in asymmetric or oligoarticular patterns. Early identification and treatment of RA with DMARDs (e.g., methotrexate) is critical to prevent permanent joint destruction and disability. Although ANA can be positive in RA, it is often negative or only weakly positive, unlike in lupus. The absence of rash, oral ulcers, and multi-system involvement further makes lupus unlikely. In sum, the symmetric small-joint arthritis, chronic course, systemic symptoms, and serologic findings make RA the most accurate diagnosis in this patient.

subject: MSK
stem: A 28-year-old man comes to the emergency department after falling off his skateboard. He has pain in his left shoulder and holds his arm slightly abducted and externally rotated. Physical exam shows decreased sensation over the lateral deltoid and weakness in shoulder abduction. An x-ray is performed; the left image shows a healthy patient and the right image shows this patient. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s acute presentation?

options.A: Axillary nerve injury; anterior shoulder dislocation
options.C: Axillary nerve injury; surgical neck of humerus fracture
options.D: Radial nerve injury; midshaft humerus fracture
options.E: Musculocutaneous nerve injury; biceps tendon rupture

answer_text: Axillary nerve injury; anterior shoulder dislocation
explanation: Holding the arm slightly abducted and externally rotated after trauma strongly suggests anterior shoulder dislocation. Sensory loss over the lateral deltoid and weakness in shoulder abduction indicate associated axillary nerve injury, the classic nerve complication of anterior glenohumeral dislocation. The radiograph comparison supports an acute dislocation rather than a fracture pattern.
bottom_line: Axillary nerve injury; anterior shoulder dislocation

subject: MSK
stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with pain along the inner side of his right elbow that has gradually worsened over the past month. He works as a construction supervisor and often lifts heavy equipment and tools. He reports that the pain becomes more noticeable when he grips objects tightly or bends his wrist downward against resistance, particularly when using a hammer or wrench. He denies any trauma, numbness, or tingling in the hand or forearm. He has no prior joint problems and is otherwise healthy. Pain is elicited when the patient flexes his wrist against resistance with the elbow fully extended. No joint swelling, erythema, or warmth is noted, and neurologic examination of the right upper limb is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Long thoracic nerve injury; winged scapula
options.E: Median nerve injury; supracondylar fracture of humerus

explanation: This patient presents with classic symptoms of medial epicondylitis, an overuse tendinopathy resulting from repetitive stress on the flexor-pronator muscle group of the forearm. His job involves gripping and repetitive wrist flexion, which can place strain on the flexor carpi radialis and pronator teres, the primary structures involved in this condition. The pain is localized to the medial epicondyle, which is the site of origin for these muscles, and is exacerbated by resisted wrist flexion, a hallmark of this diagnosis. On physical examination, this pattern is confirmed by the elicitation of pain with wrist flexion against resistance, while the elbow is extended. The absence of neurologic symptoms or joint effusion, along with preserved range of motion, helps exclude other causes such as nerve entrapment or inflammatory arthritis. His pain is mechanical and activity- related, not associated with systemic symptoms or constitutional findings. There is no swelling, erythema, or weakness on examination, supporting a localized tendinopathy. The pathology of medial epicondylitis lies in microtearing and inflammation at the tendinous origin of the wrist flexors. Repeated strain without adequate rest leads to degeneration and fibrosis, which contributes to the patient’s progressive discomfort. The most commonly affected structures are the flexor carpi radialis and pronator teres, as emphasized in this patient’s clinical setting. Early management includes conservative therapy with activity modification, physical therapy, NSAIDs, and use of a forearm counterforce strap to reduce tension at the medial epicondyle. The diagnosis is clinical and does not typically require imaging unless symptoms persist despite treatment. Surgical intervention is reserved for refractory cases that do not improve with a prolonged course of conservative therapy.

subject: MSK
stem: A 30-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that began after he twisted his knee while stepping off a curb three weeks ago. He says the pain is localized to the outer side of the knee and is aggravated by walking, especially when going up stairs or squatting. He also reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it “gets stuck” while partially bent and then suddenly releases with a click. There is no history of swelling or instability, but he avoids certain positions because of fear the knee might catch again. On physical exam, there is mild tenderness along the lateral joint line. When the leg is internally rotated while extending the flexed knee, he winces and reports pain and a clicking sensation. The remainder of the exam is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
